12. Working Priorities

Immediate priorities

  1. Convert this registry into a structured table or spreadsheet.
  2. Add links for each entry.
  3. Identify source systems vs individual resources.
  4. Add type, domain, and function to each priority entry.
  5. Add source lineage and use type.
  6. Add relationship mapping for the first 50 entries.
  7. Mark review and maturity status.
  8. Flag high-risk entries.
  9. Route entries into Encyclopedia, Option Library, Deployment, Implementation Memory, Infrastructure, or Open Questions.
  10. Create the first Source System Registry view.
  11. Create the first Ecosystem Atlas views.

Suggested first sprint

Sprint goal: Create the Minimum Viable Resource Graph.

Outputs:

  • Resource Graph table v0.1
  • Source System Registry v0.1
  • 25–50 structured priority entries
  • First relationship grammar applied to priority entries
  • High-risk resource flags
  • First routing notes into other Toolkit layers
  • Contributor form for adding new resources

Success condition:

A new contributor can add a resource in less than 10 minutes without understanding the full Toolkit architecture.

13. Open Questions

  • What is the minimum viable Resource Graph?
  • Which fields are essential now, and which should wait?
  • Which resources should become reviewed entries first?
  • Which are source systems rather than individual resources?
  • Which source systems need explicit permission, correction paths, or return paths?
  • Which entries should become Concept & Idea Ecology lineage notes?
  • Which entries should become Option Library entries?
  • Which entries are infrastructure candidates?
  • Which entries are implementation cases?
  • Which are failure cases?
  • Which are only loose leads?
  • Which resources should be removed?
  • What is missing from the atlas?
  • Which communities should be asked for correction or context?
  • Which resources require legal, ecological, privacy, or structural review before practical use?
  • What relationship types are useful enough to keep?
  • What relationship types are too complex for v0.1?
  • How should the Resource Graph eventually migrate into Markdown, CSV, JSON, a graph database, Geo, or another substrate?
  • How should changes to the Resource Graph be reviewed and logged?
  • Who is responsible for maintaining the Source System Registry?
  • How should source systems receive attribution, corrections, collaboration, or return value?

Working Summary

The Resource Graph & Ecosystem Atlas is the Toolkit’s map of reality.

It helps knowledge gardeners move from scattered resources toward a structured, relational, source-aware ecosystem map.

The Resource Graph makes resources classifiable.

The Ecosystem Atlas makes relationships interpretable.

The Source System Registry makes attribution and reciprocity visible.

The Relationship Grammar turns the inventory into a graph.

The Review & Maturity system prevents raw or risky resources from becoming false authority.

The purpose is not to collect everything.

The purpose is to help the ecosystem see itself, credit itself, connect itself, and learn from itself.

🌺 Concept & Idea Ecology

Purpose

The Concept & Idea Ecology maps the ideas, frameworks, lineages, paradigms, tensions, and language patterns that shape the Regen Knowledge Commons Toolkit.

The Toolkit should not only map organizations, tools, protocols, projects, and resources. It should also map the conceptual worlds those entities come from.

Ideas shape what people build.
Language shapes what people notice.
Frameworks shape what people treat as important.
Lineages shape what gets credited.
Tensions reveal where deeper thinking is needed.

This section helps the Toolkit understand the intellectual and cultural terrain it is moving through.

Core function

The Concept & Idea Ecology helps answer:

  • What ideas are shaping this field?
  • Where did these ideas come from?
  • Which concepts are shared across multiple ecosystems?
  • Which concepts are used differently by different communities?
  • Which ideas should be credited to specific lineages, thinkers, or source systems?
  • Which concepts are useful but vague?
  • Which tensions remain unresolved?
  • Which ideas should become Encyclopedia pages?
  • Which ideas should inform the Option Library?
  • Which ideas should shape Deployment and Structural Integrity?
  • Which ideas are missing from the current map?

Difference from the Resource Graph

The Resource Graph maps entities.

The Concept & Idea Ecology maps meaning.

Resource Graph

Concept & Idea Ecology

OpenCivics

Open civic innovation, civic infrastructure, knowledge commoning

Bloom Network

Regenerative culture, local action, community weaving

SuperBenefit

Reimagining power, impact networks, systems transformation

Crypto Altruists

Web3 impact, crypto philanthropy, nonprofit adoption

Gitcoin

Public goods funding, quadratic funding, ecosystem allocation

Regenesis

Regenerative development, place-sourced design, nested wholes

CSIS

Structural integrity, adverse signals, obligation direction

Hylo

Place-based coordination, requests and offers, community discovery

EAS

Attestations, verifiable claims, source-linked records

OpenTEAM

Agroecological data, land stewardship, open ecological tooling

The same organization, project, or tool may connect to many concepts.
The same concept may appear across many organizations, communities, and tools.

What belongs here

This section can include:

  • concepts
  • frameworks
  • intellectual lineages
  • movement language
  • paradigms
  • tensions
  • metaphors
  • design principles
  • bridge terms
  • structural terms
  • recurring questions
  • conceptual clusters
  • source lineages
  • schools of thought
  • practice traditions
  • anti-patterns
  • interpretive lenses

Concept entry template

Field

Description

Concept

Name of concept or idea.

Short definition

Plain-language explanation.

Why it matters

Why the Toolkit should care.

Related lineages

Thinkers, movements, communities, frameworks, or fields connected to it.

Related resources

Books, articles, orgs, tools, source systems, events, or cases.

Related Toolkit layers

Where this concept matters.

Common confusions

Terms or ideas it is often confused with.

Tensions / critiques

Where the concept is contested or risky.

Possible use

Encyclopedia page, Option Library entry, Deployment check, Resource Graph tag, etc.

Maturity status

Raw, draft, reviewed, source-linked, field-informed, open question.

Lightweight concept entry

For quick gardening:

Field

Description

Concept

What idea should be mapped?

Meaning

What does it mean in this context?

Source / lineage

Where did it come from?

Related concepts

What does it connect to?

Why it matters

Why should the Toolkit include it?

1. Knowledge Commoning

Core idea

Knowledge commoning is the practice of collectively stewarding knowledge as a shared resource.

It is not only content management. It includes contribution, curation, review, attribution, maintenance, access, synthesis, memory, and adaptation.

Related concepts

  • knowledge commons
  • knowledge gardening
  • source lineage
  • attribution
  • open knowledge
  • shared memory
  • public knowledge
  • digital gardens
  • component indexes
  • resource graphs
  • living documents
  • review and maturity
  • federated knowledge systems
  • decentralized curation
  • commons stewardship
  • knowledge maintenance
  • knowledge decay
  • open questions
  • synthesis
  • pattern harvesting

Why it matters

The Toolkit is fundamentally a knowledge commoning project.

Its goal is not simply to collect information. It is to help a community steward knowledge over time: making it findable, attributable, contextual, reusable, corrigible, and capable of learning from practice.

Key tensions

  • openness vs stewardship
  • contribution ease vs metadata quality
  • broad participation vs review burden
  • AI-assisted synthesis vs source fidelity
  • curation vs gatekeeping
  • decentralization vs coherence
  • living knowledge vs stable reference
  • shared memory vs information overload

Related Toolkit sections

  • Ontology
  • Encyclopedia
  • Resource Graph
  • Provenance & Attribution
  • Review & Maturity
  • Synthesis & Sensemaking
  • Evolution

2. Commons and P2P Lineages

Core idea

Commons and P2P lineages frame shared resources as collectively stewarded systems rather than purely private, state-owned, or market-managed assets.

This lineage informs how the Toolkit thinks about stewardship, participation, access, reciprocity, governance / coordination, and enclosure.

Related concepts

  • commons
  • commoning
  • peer production
  • commons transition
  • open knowledge
  • digital commons
  • platform cooperativism
  • cooperative ownership
  • mutual aid
  • enclosure
  • contribution
  • stewardship
  • polycentric governance
  • public goods
  • shared infrastructure
  • resource sovereignty

Possible lineage references

  • Elinor Ostrom
  • Michel Bauwens
  • P2P Foundation
  • Commons Transition
  • Creative Commons
  • Open Knowledge Foundation
  • platform cooperativism
  • cooperative movements
  • open-source communities
  • mutual aid networks

Why it matters

The Toolkit should not treat knowledge as content extracted from communities and reorganized into a new central platform.

Commons and P2P lineages remind the Toolkit to ask:

  • Who stewards this?
  • Who contributes?
  • Who benefits?
  • Who has access?
  • Who can correct or contest?
  • What prevents enclosure?
  • What obligations exist toward source communities?
  • What should remain distributed?

Key tensions

  • open access vs community consent
  • reuse vs extraction
  • shared stewardship vs unclear responsibility
  • commons governance vs governance theater
  • platform ownership vs commons infrastructure
  • decentralization vs abandonment
  • contribution without maintenance

Related Toolkit sections

  • Working Posture
  • Provenance & Attribution
  • Resource Graph
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Legal / Institutional Interface
  • Implementation Memory

3. Regenerative Design and Development

Core idea

Regenerative design asks how systems can increase the vitality, capacity, and evolutionary potential of the living systems they are part of.

It is not only about sustainability, restoration, or impact reduction. It asks how places, communities, ecosystems, and human systems can develop in ways that support life, relationship, learning, and renewal.

Related concepts

  • regeneration
  • regenerative development
  • living systems
  • place-sourced design
  • nested wholes
  • potential
  • reciprocity
  • developmental practice
  • ecological vitality
  • social vitality
  • regenerative economics
  • whole-systems thinking
  • local uniqueness
  • evolving capacity
  • mutualism
  • stewardship

Possible lineage references

  • Regenesis
  • Carol Sanford
  • John Fullerton / Capital Institute
  • Doughnut Economics
  • permaculture
  • agroecology
  • Indigenous and place-based knowledge systems
  • ecological restoration
  • bioregionalism
  • living systems theory

Why it matters

The Toolkit should not only organize regenerative knowledge. It should try to behave regeneratively as a knowledge system.

That means:

  • preserving source lineage
  • avoiding extraction
  • respecting place and context
  • supporting local capacity
  • valuing living knowledge
  • avoiding false universality
  • learning from implementation
  • recognizing affected ecosystems and communities
  • treating knowledge as relational, not only informational

Key tensions

  • regenerative language vs extractive practice
  • global frameworks vs local specificity
  • ecological complexity vs dashboard metrics
  • impact claims vs lived context
  • financialization vs stewardship
  • abstraction vs place
  • scaling vs deepening
  • knowledge sharing vs knowledge extraction

Related Toolkit sections

  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Ecology / MRV
  • Localism and Bioregionalism
  • Implementation Memory
  • Review & Maturity

4. Public Goods and Web3 Coordination

Core idea

Public goods are resources that are non-excludable and non-rivalrous, or at least widely beneficial and difficult to fund through ordinary market mechanisms.

Web3 public goods work explores how cryptographic infrastructure, programmable coordination, funding mechanisms, attestations, and decentralized communities can support public goods creation and maintenance.

Related concepts

  • public goods
  • digital public goods
  • protocol public goods
  • open-source infrastructure
  • ecosystem funding
  • retroactive funding
  • quadratic funding
  • grants
  • attestations
  • hypercerts
  • onchain records
  • credible neutrality
  • mechanism design
  • coordination failure
  • commons infrastructure
  • contribution records
  • impact claims
  • public goods funding rounds

Possible lineage references

  • Ethereum
  • Gitcoin
  • Optimism Retro Funding
  • Allo
  • Octant
  • Giveth
  • Open Collective
  • Hypercerts
  • Ethereum Attestation Service
  • Open Source Observer
  • public goods economics
  • open-source communities
  • mechanism design research

Why it matters

The Toolkit emerges partly from regenerative web3 and public goods ecosystems. These ecosystems provide many of the tools, mechanisms, and questions that shape the project.

Important questions include:

  • How should public goods be funded?
  • How should contributions be recognized?
  • How should impact be claimed?
  • How should grants be reported?
  • How can funding avoid gaming and capture?
  • How can open-source infrastructure be maintained?
  • How can local impact connect with global funding systems?
  • How can regenerative projects avoid being reduced to financial primitives?

Key tensions

  • public goods funding vs grant dependency
  • impact claims vs evidence quality
  • token incentives vs intrinsic motivation
  • retroactive rewards vs future capacity
  • open-source maintenance vs unpaid labor
  • ecosystem funding vs ecosystem capture
  • metrics vs meaning
  • local impact vs global funder expectations

Related Toolkit sections

  • Resource Graph
  • Option Library
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Implementation Memory
  • Funding and Allocation
  • Review & Maturity
  • Web3 Infrastructure

5. ReFi and Regenerative Crypto

Core idea

Regenerative finance and regenerative crypto explore how financial, social, technical, and ecological systems can be designed to support regeneration rather than extraction.

In web3 contexts, ReFi often includes climate finance, ecological assets, impact claims, local currencies, public goods funding, natural capital, community currencies, ecological data, and place-based coordination.

Related concepts

  • regenerative finance
  • ReFi
  • regenerative crypto
  • ecological assets
  • climate finance
  • carbon markets
  • biodiversity credits
  • local currencies
  • community currencies
  • vouchers
  • mutual credit
  • bioregional finance
  • impact claims
  • MRV
  • ecological data
  • local regenerative economies
  • public goods funding
  • regenerative coordination
  • web3 for local impact

Possible lineage references

  • ReFi DAO
  • GreenPill Network
  • Celo
  • Regen Network
  • Climate Collective
  • Toucan
  • Open Forest Protocol
  • Silvi Protocol
  • Grassroots Economics / Sarafu
  • localism finance work
  • regenerative economics
  • ecological restoration
  • climate justice

Why it matters

The Toolkit is partly designed to help regenerative web3 communities discover resources, coordinate work, learn from implementation, and connect local action with broader public goods and funding ecosystems.

ReFi is relevant, but should not narrow the Toolkit into finance alone. The Toolkit should keep regeneration, community coordination, ecological stewardship, and knowledge commoning at the center.

Key tensions

  • regeneration vs financialization
  • ecological complexity vs tradable assets
  • local value vs global markets
  • community benefit vs investor logic
  • verification burden vs grassroots participation
  • carbon tunnel vision vs whole ecosystems
  • speculative incentives vs long-term stewardship
  • funding flows vs real implementation capacity

Related Toolkit sections

  • Resource Graph
  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Ecology / MRV
  • Localism and Bioregionalism
  • Funding and Allocation
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Implementation Memory

6. Localism, Bioregionalism, and Place-Based Coordination

Core idea

Localism and bioregionalism emphasize that coordination should be grounded in place, ecology, community, culture, and local context.

A local node is not only a branch of a larger network. It is a situated coordination system with its own relationships, needs, assets, constraints, histories, and ecological realities.

Related concepts

  • local nodes
  • chapters
  • hubs
  • cells
  • bioregions
  • watersheds
  • place-based coordination
  • local autonomy
  • translocal learning
  • local resource flows
  • community wealth
  • mutual aid
  • solidarity economy
  • municipalism
  • civic infrastructure
  • community currencies
  • local stewardship
  • community discovery
  • place profile
  • local legitimacy

Possible lineage references

  • Bloom Network
  • GreenPill chapters
  • ReFi DAO Localism Finance
  • Hylo
  • Bioregional Learning Centre
  • Cooperative Jackson
  • New Economy Coalition
  • Grassroots Economics / Sarafu
  • Fearless Cities
  • Transition-town-like movements
  • municipalist movements
  • Indigenous and local knowledge systems

Why it matters

The original motivation for the Toolkit includes helping local regenerative web3 communities operate more effectively.

Local groups need more than concepts. They need ways to:

  • find relevant knowledge
  • map local resources
  • coordinate people
  • discover collaborators
  • connect needs and offers
  • track projects
  • access funding
  • preserve local memory
  • learn from other places
  • avoid importing inappropriate models
  • represent themselves safely and accurately

Key tensions

  • local autonomy vs network coherence
  • local specificity vs reusable patterns
  • mapping communities vs exposing them
  • global web3 tools vs local realities
  • imported solutions vs place-sourced design
  • local legitimacy vs external funding
  • volunteer energy vs operational burden
  • translocal learning vs flattening context

Related Toolkit sections

  • Resource Graph & Ecosystem Atlas
  • Discovery & Affinity
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Implementation Memory
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Place Profiles

7. Civic Innovation and Democratic Infrastructure

Core idea

Civic innovation explores how communities, institutions, networks, and technologies can support collective decision-making, participation, public problem-solving, and civic life.

Democratic infrastructure includes the tools, processes, norms, institutions, and data systems that enable people to deliberate, decide, coordinate, and act together.

Related concepts

  • civic infrastructure
  • open civic innovation
  • participatory democracy
  • public interest technology
  • civic tech
  • deliberation
  • digital democracy
  • municipal innovation
  • public dialogue
  • participatory budgeting
  • open government
  • civic data
  • digital public infrastructure
  • community governance
  • citizen science
  • public knowledge
  • civic imagination

Possible lineage references

  • OpenCivics
  • Decidim
  • Pol.is
  • vTaiwan
  • Consul Democracy
  • Your Priorities
  • Open Civic Data
  • Open Referral
  • Metagov
  • public interest technology
  • civic AI efforts
  • municipalist movements

Why it matters

The Toolkit overlaps strongly with civic infrastructure because it is concerned with how people discover knowledge, coordinate resources, make decisions, preserve memory, and learn together.

Civic innovation also helps keep the Toolkit from becoming overly web3-native. Many of the problems the Toolkit cares about existed before web3 and have been explored deeply by civic technologists, public sector innovators, deliberation practitioners, and democratic infrastructure builders.

Key tensions

  • participation vs participation theater
  • digital access vs digital exclusion
  • civic legitimacy vs platform control
  • open participation vs moderation burden
  • public transparency vs privacy
  • deliberation vs decision fatigue
  • institutional partnership vs capture
  • civic tech enthusiasm vs maintenance reality

Related Toolkit sections

  • Resource Graph
  • Option Library
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Infrastructure & Interoperability
  • Implementation Memory
  • Concept & Idea Ecology

8. Governance, Coordination, and Structural Integrity

Core idea

Governance and coordination are often used loosely. The Toolkit should use familiar bridge language where helpful, but become more precise where structure matters.

Coordination architecture refers to the roles, decision paths, obligations, information flows, consent conditions, power/control points, review processes, and response pathways that allow people to act together.

Structural integrity asks whether those arrangements are clear enough, fair enough, responsive enough, and context-aware enough to function without hidden harm.

Related concepts

  • governance
  • coordination architecture
  • coordination instruments
  • roles
  • decision paths
  • stewardship obligation
  • obligation direction
  • information asymmetry
  • power and control
  • consent legibility
  • adverse signals
  • detection with named response
  • structural floors
  • generative capacities
  • review pathways
  • conformance
  • conflict transformation
  • cost-bearing parties
  • scale thresholds
  • trust radius
  • contributor capacity

Possible lineage references

  • CSIS
  • Metagov
  • DAOstar
  • Govbase
  • Sociocracy
  • Holacracy
  • Viable System Model
  • Ostrom
  • cybernetics
  • organizational design
  • digital governance research
  • civic deliberation

Why it matters

The Toolkit may influence funding, local coordination, knowledge representation, AI-assisted discovery, public claims, community profiles, and ecological work.

That means it needs more than vague governance language. It needs clear structures for:

  • who decides
  • who maintains
  • who reviews
  • who can contest
  • who bears costs
  • who controls infrastructure
  • who has hidden information
  • who responds to harm
  • what happens when something fails

Bridge language and structural language

Bridge language

More precise structural language

Governance

Coordination architecture / coordination instrument

Accountability

Stewardship obligation / obligation direction

Stakeholders

Participants / contributors / cost-bearing parties

Transparency

Multi-directional visibility

Enforcement

Detection with named response

Compliance

Conformance

Feedback

Signal / adverse signal / learning signal

Users

Participants / contributors / affected parties

Power structure

Power, control, and obligation structure

Rules

Structural conditions

Key tensions

  • governance as language vs governance as structure
  • visible rules vs hidden influence
  • consent vs passive participation
  • transparency vs information overload
  • decentralization vs accountability gaps
  • autonomy vs coherence
  • speed vs legitimacy
  • conflict avoidance vs conflict transformation
  • formal power vs informal power

Related Toolkit sections

  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Option Library
  • Review & Maturity
  • Evolution
  • Resource Graph
  • Implementation Memory

9. AI, Collective Intelligence, and Sensemaking

Core idea

AI and collective intelligence tools can help people search, summarize, classify, map, compare, synthesize, and discover patterns across large bodies of knowledge.

But AI can also create false authority, flatten context, erase source lineage, and amplify semantic drift.

The Toolkit should use AI as an assistant to knowledge gardening, not as a replacement for stewardship.

Related concepts

  • collective intelligence
  • distributed cognition
  • sensemaking
  • AI-assisted curation
  • AI-assisted synthesis
  • semantic search
  • embeddings
  • retrieval-augmented generation
  • human-in-the-loop review
  • deliberation
  • pattern recognition
  • epistemic commons
  • model-mediated interpretation
  • automation bias
  • source fidelity
  • provenance under AI use
  • disagreement preservation
  • narrative capture

Possible lineage references

  • Collective Intelligence Project
  • Harmonica
  • Bonfires
  • Pol.is
  • Kialo
  • SenseMaker
  • OpenMined
  • civic AI projects
  • public interest AI
  • digital garden tools
  • AI safety and governance research

Why it matters

The Toolkit will likely rely on AI for some combination of:

  • resource classification
  • concept extraction
  • synthesis
  • search
  • comparison
  • relationship mapping
  • glossary support
  • draft generation
  • pattern detection
  • review assistance
  • contributor support

This can make the commons more usable, but only if source lineage, human review, and uncertainty remain visible.

Key tensions

  • speed vs trust
  • synthesis vs flattening
  • pattern detection vs hallucination
  • AI recommendations vs consent
  • semantic search vs source context
  • polished drafts vs review maturity
  • automation vs stewardship
  • model interpretation vs community interpretation
  • scale vs nuance

Related Toolkit sections

  • Synthesis & Sensemaking
  • Review & Maturity
  • Resource Graph
  • Infrastructure & Interoperability
  • Discovery & Affinity
  • Evolution
  • Privacy, Consent & Identity

10. Ecology, MRV, and Evidence

Core idea

Ecological and regenerative work requires evidence, but ecological evidence is complex, context-sensitive, and often difficult to reduce to simple metrics.

MRV, monitoring, community science, remote sensing, field records, attestations, and ecological indicators can support accountability, but they can also distort reality if treated as complete representations of living systems.

Related concepts

  • ecology
  • biodiversity
  • restoration
  • soil health
  • water cycles
  • carbon cycles
  • agroecology
  • community science
  • MRV
  • monitoring
  • reporting
  • verification
  • remote sensing
  • field evidence
  • ecological indicators
  • data quality
  • carbon tunnel vision
  • biodiversity blind spots
  • local ecological knowledge
  • environmental claims
  • impact verification
  • restoration stewardship

Possible lineage references

  • OpenTEAM
  • farmOS
  • Restor
  • GBIF
  • iNaturalist
  • Global Forest Watch
  • OpenMRV
  • Plan Vivo
  • Verra
  • Gold Standard
  • Regen Network
  • Open Forest Protocol
  • Silvi Protocol
  • local ecological knowledge systems

Why it matters

The Toolkit may help communities make or evaluate ecological claims.

That creates a high standard of care. Environmental claims should not be treated as simple marketing, grant reporting, or tokenization material.

The Toolkit should help distinguish:

  • claim
  • evidence
  • measurement
  • observation
  • verification
  • attestation
  • interpretation
  • uncertainty
  • ecological outcome
  • social outcome
  • financial outcome

Key tensions

  • evidence vs over-measurement
  • local knowledge vs external standards
  • community science vs expert verification
  • carbon metrics vs ecosystem complexity
  • remote sensing vs field reality
  • ecological restoration vs asset creation
  • dashboards vs living systems
  • verification burden vs grassroots participation
  • claims vs stewardship

Related Toolkit sections

  • Ecology / MRV
  • Review & Maturity
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Implementation Memory
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Resource Graph
  • Option Library

11. Local Finance, Community Currencies, and Resource Flows

Core idea

Regenerative coordination depends on more than grants or token funding. It also involves local resource flows: time, labor, care, food, tools, space, land, trust, knowledge, materials, and community currencies.

Local finance asks how value can circulate in ways that strengthen communities rather than extract from them.

Related concepts

  • local currencies
  • community currencies
  • mutual credit
  • vouchers
  • commitment pooling
  • local resource flows
  • solidarity economy
  • cooperative finance
  • community wealth
  • gift economies
  • reciprocal exchange
  • time banking
  • regenerative capital
  • bioregional finance
  • funding pools
  • liquidity pools
  • treasury stewardship
  • non-financial capital
  • eight forms of capital

Possible lineage references

  • Grassroots Economics / Sarafu
  • ReFi DAO Localism Finance
  • community currency movements
  • mutual credit systems
  • solidarity economy networks
  • cooperative finance
  • local economic development
  • bioregional finance
  • regenerative economics
  • GreenPill local experiments

Why it matters

Local regenerative communities often need ways to coordinate value before, during, and after formal funding.

The Toolkit should help communities think beyond grants:

  • What resources already exist locally?
  • What forms of contribution matter?
  • What can be exchanged?
  • What should be gifted?
  • What should be funded?
  • What should be recognized?
  • What should not be financialized?
  • How can value circulate locally?
  • What infrastructure supports this safely?

Key tensions

  • local circulation vs external capital
  • tokens vs trust
  • funding vs dependency
  • liquidity vs speculation
  • recognition vs commodification
  • informal exchange vs legal obligations
  • local value vs global markets
  • mutual aid vs administrative burden

Related Toolkit sections

  • Option Library
  • Localism and Bioregionalism
  • Funding and Allocation
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Implementation Memory
  • Legal / Institutional Interface

12. Legal, Institutional, and Civic Interface

Core idea

Regenerative web3 and knowledge commons projects often operate between informal community coordination, digital infrastructure, legal entities, funders, public institutions, and real-world obligations.

The legal and institutional interface helps clarify what forms, obligations, risks, and constraints matter when ideas become operational.

Related concepts

  • nonprofit
  • cooperative
  • association
  • fiscal sponsorship
  • foundation
  • company
  • DAO
  • DAO legal wrapper
  • steward ownership
  • land trust
  • data trust
  • public-private partnership
  • municipal interface
  • compliance
  • conformance
  • liability
  • tax obligations
  • public accountability
  • civic partnership
  • computable law
  • licensing
  • open-source license
  • Creative Commons license

Possible lineage references

  • Open Collective
  • Creative Commons
  • Platform Cooperativism Consortium
  • Purpose Foundation
  • LexDAO
  • Stanford CodeX
  • computable law research
  • cooperative law
  • nonprofit policy resources
  • public sector innovation
  • DAO legal research

Why it matters

The Toolkit may support real deployments involving money, public claims, local communities, ecological work, identity, data, or governance / coordination processes. Those deployments may have legal, tax, fiscal, or institutional implications.

The Toolkit should not provide legal advice by default. It should help identify when legal or institutional review is needed.

Key tensions

  • informal practice vs formal obligation
  • DAO language vs legal reality
  • open knowledge vs licensing clarity
  • public claims vs liability
  • local action vs jurisdictional constraints
  • fiscal sponsorship vs autonomy
  • civic partnership vs institutional capture
  • compliance language vs structural conformance

Related Toolkit sections

  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Option Library
  • Review & Maturity
  • Resource Graph
  • Funding and Allocation
  • Privacy, Consent & Identity

13. Culture, Narrative, and Social Practice

Core idea

Culture is infrastructure.

A knowledge commons is not held together only by tools, schemas, docs, and processes. It is also held by language, rituals, stories, norms, trust, recognition, shared memory, conflict practices, and contributor care.

Related concepts

  • culture
  • narrative
  • movement language
  • rituals
  • gatherings
  • trust
  • belonging
  • social infrastructure
  • community memory
  • memes
  • symbols
  • storytelling
  • onboarding
  • recognition
  • contributor care
  • maintenance labor
  • invisible labor
  • burnout
  • repair
  • celebration
  • seasonal rhythms
  • relational coordination

Possible lineage references

  • Bloom Network
  • emergent strategy
  • adrienne maree brown
  • regenerative culture movements
  • community organizing traditions
  • ritual and facilitation traditions
  • local community practices
  • web3 public goods culture
  • GreenPill culture
  • open-source culture
  • Pattern Language

Why it matters

Many coordination systems fail not because the mechanism is wrong, but because the culture cannot hold it.

The Toolkit should help people notice:

  • how contributors are welcomed
  • how knowledge is credited
  • how disagreements are handled
  • how invisible labor is recognized
  • how burnout is prevented
  • how local context is respected
  • how stories travel
  • how community memory is preserved
  • how rituals support coordination

Key tensions

  • vibe vs structure
  • openness vs belonging
  • rituals vs empty performance
  • recognition vs status games
  • culture vs cult dynamics
  • narrative coherence vs propaganda
  • contributor care vs productivity pressure
  • community warmth vs conflict avoidance
  • movement language vs insider jargon

Related Toolkit sections

  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Implementation Memory
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Discovery & Affinity
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Contributor Pathways

14. Open Research, DeSci, and Public Knowledge

Core idea

Open research and DeSci explore how knowledge production can become more open, participatory, transparent, funded, reproducible, and publicly useful.

The Toolkit can learn from research commons, evidence mapping, citation graphs, public repositories, peer review, research bounties, and collaborative synthesis.

Related concepts

  • open science
  • DeSci
  • public knowledge
  • research commons
  • peer review
  • open peer review
  • reproducibility
  • research funding
  • research bounties
  • research sprints
  • literature review
  • evidence mapping
  • citation graphs
  • open data
  • knowledge synthesis
  • claim-evidence mapping
  • public research infrastructure

Possible lineage references

  • DeSci World
  • OpenAlex
  • ResearchHub
  • Zenodo
  • OurResearch
  • Open Knowledge Foundation
  • OCC Research
  • academic commons
  • public research repositories
  • citizen science
  • community science

Why it matters

The Toolkit needs better ways to distinguish:

  • claims
  • evidence
  • synthesis
  • opinion
  • field notes
  • implementation learning
  • research findings
  • unresolved questions

Research practices can help the Toolkit improve its review systems, source lineage, evidence quality, and public knowledge standards.

Key tensions

  • academic rigor vs practical usability
  • open research vs extractive publication systems
  • citation counts vs public value
  • peer review vs community review
  • expert knowledge vs lived experience
  • evidence standards vs field complexity
  • research bounties vs shallow outputs
  • AI literature synthesis vs source fidelity

Related Toolkit sections

  • Encyclopedia
  • Review & Maturity
  • Resource Graph
  • Synthesis & Sensemaking
  • Implementation Memory
  • Open Questions & Research Backlog

15. Privacy, Consent, and Cypherpunk Lineages

Core idea

Privacy and consent are not optional technical features. They are structural conditions for safe participation, especially when a system maps people, communities, locations, skills, needs, offers, contribution histories, or local projects.

Cypherpunk and privacy lineages remind the Toolkit that digital coordination can become surveillance if visibility, identity, and matching are handled carelessly.

Related concepts

  • privacy
  • consent
  • encryption
  • anonymity
  • pseudonymity
  • selective disclosure
  • zero-knowledge proofs
  • identity sovereignty
  • metadata protection
  • secure communication
  • location privacy
  • opt-in discovery
  • data minimization
  • community safety
  • surveillance risk
  • privacy-preserving AI
  • privacy-preserving matching

Possible lineage references

  • Signal
  • Web3Privacy Now
  • 0xPARC
  • Nym
  • Aztec
  • OpenMined
  • cypherpunk research
  • privacy engineering
  • decentralized identity work
  • secure communication communities

Why it matters

The Toolkit’s Discovery & Affinity ambitions create privacy risks.

A system that helps people find each other may also expose:

  • sensitive locations
  • vulnerable communities
  • local organizers
  • funding needs
  • political or ecological conflicts
  • informal networks
  • contribution histories
  • personal interests
  • relationship graphs

The Toolkit should design discovery with consent from the beginning.

Key tensions

  • discoverability vs safety
  • visibility vs consent
  • identity vs pseudonymity
  • matching vs surveillance
  • open maps vs sensitive places
  • contribution history vs privacy
  • reputation vs exclusion
  • AI recommendations vs consent
  • public commons vs protected knowledge

Related Toolkit sections

  • Privacy, Consent & Identity
  • Discovery & Affinity
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Resource Graph
  • Infrastructure & Interoperability
  • Regenerative Obligation

16. Failure, Anti-Patterns, and Structural Drift

Core idea

Failure should be part of the knowledge commons.

The Toolkit should preserve not only best practices, but also recurring breakdowns, harmful patterns, weak structures, incentive failures, coordination breakdowns, and examples of drift.

Failure knowledge helps communities avoid repeating harm.

Related concepts

  • anti-patterns
  • failure modes
  • structural drift
  • governance theater
  • extractive aggregation
  • documentation decay
  • local node drift
  • grant dependency
  • contributor burnout
  • hidden power
  • capture
  • metric gaming
  • token speculation
  • ecological overclaiming
  • AI flattening
  • weak consent
  • weak source lineage
  • implementation overload
  • coordination collapse

Possible lineage references

  • failure case studies
  • DAO failures
  • open-source maintainer burnout literature
  • commons enclosure analysis
  • organizational failure cases
  • governance case forums
  • local project retrospectives
  • public goods funding postmortems

Why it matters

A good Toolkit should not only say what to do. It should help people notice when something is going wrong.

Failure and anti-pattern mapping can inform:

  • Deployment checks
  • Option Library risk notes
  • Review & Maturity states
  • Implementation retrospectives
  • Evolution updates
  • contributor care
  • structural integrity reviews

Key tensions

  • learning from failure vs blame
  • public transparency vs reputational harm
  • naming patterns vs exposing people
  • failure analysis vs cynicism
  • critique vs care
  • unresolved tension vs forced closure

Related Toolkit sections

  • Implementation Memory
  • Evolution
  • Deployment & Structural Integrity
  • Option Library
  • Review & Maturity
  • Regenerative Obligation

Cross-cutting conceptual tensions

The Toolkit should preserve tensions rather than forcing premature resolution.

Open vs protected

Some knowledge should be open. Some should be contextual. Some should be protected. Some should not be mapped publicly.

Local vs global

Reusable patterns are useful, but local context matters. A pattern that works in one place may fail elsewhere.

Broad vs coherent

The Toolkit should be broad enough to hold the field, but coherent enough to navigate.

AI speed vs human stewardship

AI can accelerate curation and synthesis, but human review and source lineage remain essential.

Finance vs regeneration

Funding is necessary, but regenerative coordination should not collapse into financial mechanisms.

Evidence vs living systems

Evidence matters, but living systems cannot be fully represented by metrics.

Discovery vs surveillance

Helping people find each other is powerful, but unsafe visibility can create harm.

Structure vs emergence

Structure helps coordination. Too much structure can block emergence. Too little structure can hide power and overburden informal labor.

Commons vs enclosure

Shared knowledge can become enclosed by platforms, funders, brands, or extractive aggregators.

Participation vs capacity

Open contribution is valuable, but contribution systems must account for burnout, invisible labor, and maintenance burden.

Standardization vs plurality

Interoperability requires shared structures, but different communities need different languages, practices, and contexts.

Concept-to-layer routing

Concept family

Working priorities for this section

1. Turn key concepts into Encyclopedia pages

Priority candidates:

  • knowledge commons
  • knowledge gardening
  • resource graph
  • source lineage
  • regenerative web3
  • local node
  • implementation memory
  • structural integrity
  • information asymmetry
  • stewardship obligation
  • public goods funding
  • MRV
  • discovery and affinity
  • AI-assisted curation
  • extractive aggregation

2. Build lineage maps

Useful first lineage maps:

  • commons / P2P lineage
  • regenerative design lineage
  • web3 public goods lineage
  • localism / bioregionalism lineage
  • civic innovation lineage
  • AI / collective intelligence lineage
  • structural integrity / CSIS lineage
  • ReFi / climate web3 lineage

3. Add bridge-language notes

Terms that need bridge-language treatment:

  • governance
  • accountability
  • stakeholders
  • transparency
  • enforcement
  • compliance
  • impact
  • users
  • feedback
  • community
  • ecosystem
  • decentralization
  • regeneration

4. Map tensions explicitly

Some tensions should become open-question pages or anti-pattern pages.

Examples:

  • open knowledge vs protected knowledge
  • public maps vs community safety
  • AI synthesis vs source fidelity
  • regenerative finance vs financialization
  • local specificity vs reusable patterns
  • evidence quality vs participation burden
  • contribution openness vs review quality

5. Connect concepts to resources

Each major concept should eventually link to:

  • source systems
  • key thinkers
  • relevant organizations
  • related tools
  • implementation cases
  • failure cases
  • option entries
  • open questions

Working summary

The Concept & Idea Ecology helps the Toolkit see the invisible architecture of the field: the ideas, lineages, tensions, metaphors, and assumptions that shape what people build.

A strong ecosystem map should include organizations and tools.
A deeper ecosystem map should also include concepts and lineages.

This section helps ensure that the Toolkit does not only ask:

Who is doing what?

It also asks:

What ideas are moving through the field, where did they come from, what do they make possible, and what risks do they carry?

📚 Option Library

Purpose

The Option Library organizes reusable implementation choices that communities, builders, knowledge gardeners, funders, local organizers, and ecosystem stewards can compare before moving into deployment.

It is not a resource list, tool directory, recommendation engine, or catalog of projects.

It is a structured design space for choices that can be selected, adapted, combined, tested, rejected, or refined in context.

A useful option entry should help someone understand:

  • what reusable choice the option represents;
  • what problem, gap, or tension it addresses;
  • when it may be useful;
  • when it may be risky, unnecessary, or misleading;
  • what roles, conditions, tools, and knowledge it requires;
  • what failure modes to watch for;
  • what consent, power, privacy, or regenerative obligation concerns it creates;
  • what evidence, cases, or source lineages support it;
  • how it connects to deployment and implementation learning.

The Option Library should not tell people what to choose. It should help them understand what they are choosing.

Core Distinction: Option, Tool, Resource, Deployment, Implementation

The Option Library must preserve the distinction between different object types.

Object

Meaning

Example

Concept

An idea or explanatory construct.

Source lineage, structural integrity, regenerative obligation

Resource

A source, reference, artifact, dataset, report, guide, or source system.

CSIS guide, SuperBenefit Knowledge Garden, Gitcoin Governance Forum

Tool

A specific platform, protocol, app, template, or component.

Karma GAP, Charmverse, EAS, Hypercerts, Kumu, Hylo

Option

A reusable design choice, pattern, mechanism, or pathway.

Milestone-based funding, source lineage field, opt-in discovery profile

Deployment

A context-specific assembly of options, tools, roles, obligations, and operating conditions.

A local grants round using milestone-based funding, community review, public reports, and a shared treasury

Implementation

What actually happens when a deployment is used in practice.

The actual funding round, including proposals, decisions, updates, conflicts, delays, lessons, and adaptations

A tool may support an option, but it is not itself the option.

A resource may inform an option, but it is not itself the option.

A deployment may combine several options, but it is not itself the option.

An implementation may generate learning about an option, but one case does not automatically become a reusable pattern.

What Counts as an Option

An entry belongs in the Option Library when it represents a reusable design choice or pathway that could be adapted across more than one context.

An option may be:

  • a funding mechanism;
  • a decision process;
  • a documentation pattern;
  • a discovery pattern;
  • a contribution model;
  • a review process;
  • an identity or access pattern;
  • a mapping approach;
  • a governance or coordination structure;
  • an implementation learning pattern;
  • an infrastructure or publishing pattern;
  • a measurement or evidence approach;
  • a contributor care practice;
  • a legal or institutional design choice.

An entry should not remain in the Option Library if it is primarily:

If the entry is primarily...

Move or route it to...

a tool profile

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

a raw link

Resource Graph

a source system

Source System Registry

a concept explanation

Encyclopedia or Concept & Idea Ecology

a framework explanation

Encyclopedia or Concept & Idea Ecology

a specific project or example

Implementation Memory or Case Library

an operational checklist

Deployment & Structural Integrity

a schema or object type

Ontology

a field signal

Evolution

an unresolved question

Open Questions / Research Backlog

an outdated or weak suggestion

Archive / Deprecated References

The Option Library can link to all of these layers, but it should not absorb their functions.

Option Status Labels

Each option should have one visible status label.

Status

Meaning

Raw

Named as a possible option, not yet structured.

Candidate

Likely useful, but needs review, source lineage, or clearer conditions.

Draft

Developed enough to discuss and compare.

Reviewed

Checked by relevant human reviewers.

Field-informed

Informed by one or more real implementations.

Pattern-generating

Has produced reusable lessons across contexts.

High-risk

Requires stronger review before practical use.

Deprecated

Preserved for memory, but no longer recommended or current.

Open question

Not yet stable enough to treat as an option.

A polished option should not automatically be treated as reliable. Status should reflect evidence, review, and practical maturity.

Option Entry Template

Field

Description

Option name

Name of the reusable design choice.

Short description

What the option is.

Option family

Knowledge commons, mapping, governance, funding, discovery, evidence, AI, infrastructure, legal, culture, etc.

Problem addressed

What need, gap, or tension this responds to.

Useful when

Conditions where the option may fit.

Not useful when

Conditions where the option may fail, overcomplicate, distort, or create risk.

Required roles

Stewards, reviewers, facilitators, maintainers, signers, contributors, etc.

Required infrastructure

Tools, platforms, data structures, channels, repositories, contracts, forms, etc.

Related tools

Specific tools or protocols that may support the option.

Related concepts

Concepts needed to understand the option.

Related resources

Source systems, reports, guides, frameworks, or references.

Related cases

Implementations, examples, or failures.

Risks / failure modes

What can go wrong.

Consent / privacy notes

Representation, data, identity, location, or matching concerns.

Power / control notes

Who controls resources, infrastructure, decisions, data, or narratives.

Regenerative obligation notes

Who should be credited, protected, consulted, supported, or given a correction path.

Evidence quality

What supports this option and how strong the evidence is.

Deployment requirements

What must be made explicit before use.

Maturity status

Raw, candidate, draft, reviewed, field-informed, pattern-generating, high-risk, deprecated.

Source lineage

Where the option comes from or what source systems informed it.

Open questions

What remains unresolved.

Option Families

The following families preserve the conceptual range of the previous Option Library while avoiding tool/resource drift.

Each family should eventually contain a short index, several full option entries, links to supporting tools/resources, and implementation memory from real cases.

1. Knowledge Commons Options

These options help a group create, maintain, review, publish, and evolve shared knowledge.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Public knowledge base

Shared place for concepts, guides, resources, and working memory.

Google Docs, Notion, GitHub, Astro, Starlight, Quartz

Becomes outdated, centralized, or hard to navigate.

Candidate

Digital garden

Living, linked, evolving knowledge space.

Obsidian, Quartz, Logseq, Foam, SuperBenefit Knowledge Garden

Too personal, weak review, hard for newcomers.

Candidate

Component index

Structured catalog of reusable components, templates, and patterns.

Knowledge Commoning Swarm, Resource Graph, DAOstar OpenGrants

Becomes a directory without context.

Candidate

Source lineage field

Metadata field preserving source origin, attribution, and use type.

W3C PROV, Dublin Core, Markdown frontmatter

Treated as bureaucracy instead of obligation.

Draft

Glossary

Shared definitions for key terms.

Markdown, docs, wiki, web glossary tools

False precision; contested terms hidden.

Candidate

Open-question page

Dedicated page for unresolved questions.

Docs, GitHub issues, Notion database

Questions pile up without synthesis.

Candidate

Review and maturity system

Visible states for trust, review, and readiness.

GitHub PRs, Notion status fields, Airtable, labels

Creates false authority if poorly maintained.

Draft

Contribution guide

Instructions for adding or improving knowledge.

GitHub, docs, community guides

Too complex for casual contributors.

Candidate

Knowledge maintenance rhythm

Regular practice of pruning, updating, archiving, and resurfacing.

Monthly calls, review sprints, changelogs

Invisible labor and burnout.

Candidate

AI-assisted draft with human review

Uses AI for initial synthesis while preserving human stewardship.

LLMs, RAG workflows, GitHub review

False authority, source loss, hallucination.

High-risk candidate

Pattern harvest

Turns repeated implementation lessons into reusable patterns.

Retrospectives, implementation notes, pattern templates

Overgeneralizes from weak evidence.

Candidate

Source system registry

Identifies living knowledge environments that require attribution and return paths.

Wikis, forums, GitHub repos, public libraries, knowledge gardens

Treats source systems as extractable link pools.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Source Lineage Field

Short description

A metadata pattern that records where knowledge, resources, concepts, claims, options, or implementation lessons came from and how they are being used.

Problem addressed

Knowledge commons often aggregate ideas, resources, and practices without preserving authorship, community context, source systems, or use type. This creates attribution loss, extractive aggregation, false authority, and weak trust.

Useful when

  • The Toolkit summarizes, adapts, synthesizes, or cites external work.
  • A resource comes from a living source system such as a wiki, forum, map, repository, or knowledge garden.
  • A concept, option, or case is influenced by a specific community, framework, person, event, or implementation.

Not useful when

  • A note is purely private, temporary, or not intended to enter the commons.
  • The contribution is too raw to classify. In that case, mark it Raw or To-place first.

Required roles

Source lineage steward, resource gardener, reviewer, contributor.

Required infrastructure

A source field, citation or URL field, use type field, review status field, and correction or return-path field for high-value source systems.

Related tools

GitHub, Markdown frontmatter, Zotero, Airtable, Notion, Google Sheets, W3C PROV-inspired fields, Dublin Core-inspired metadata.

Related concepts

Provenance, attribution, source system, regenerative obligation, commons stewardship, extractive aggregation, review and maturity.

Risks / failure modes

  • Source lineage becomes a checkbox instead of a practice of care.
  • Contributors avoid adding resources because the process feels too heavy.
  • Source systems are credited generically but not meaningfully linked back.
  • AI-generated summaries lose original source context.
  • Attribution privileges aggregators over original communities or knowledge holders.

Consent / privacy notes

If the source includes people, communities, exact locations, local ecological knowledge, or sensitive internal notes, source lineage should include visibility and reuse limits.

Regenerative obligation notes

Source lineage is not just citation. It is a minimal obligation toward contributors, source communities, knowledge holders, and future users.

Deployment requirements

Define required fields, optional fields, who fills them, and how corrections can be submitted.

Maturity status

Draft / Priority option

Open questions

  • What is the minimum viable source lineage field set?
  • Which entries require full source lineage before publication?
  • How should source systems receive return paths?

Priority Entry: Review and Maturity System

Short description

A visible status system that helps readers understand whether content, resources, options, claims, and deployment templates are raw, draft, reviewed, field-informed, high-risk, or deprecated.

Problem addressed

Large living documents often contain uneven material. Without visible maturity states, polished drafts may look authoritative, raw notes may be mistaken for decisions, and high-risk content may be reused without review.

Useful when

  • A knowledge commons contains mixed content states.
  • AI-assisted content is present.
  • Some content involves legal, ecological, governance, funding, privacy, or public claims.
  • Contributors need to know where to improve, review, archive, or rely on material.

Not useful when

  • The system is so small and informal that maturity labels add more overhead than clarity.
  • Labels are added but never reviewed or updated.

Required roles

Reviewer, domain reviewer, source reviewer, option librarian, deployment steward, knowledge steward.

Required infrastructure

Status labels, review type labels, high-risk triggers, review queue or review notes, and optional changelog.

Related tools

GitHub PRs, Notion status fields, Airtable, Google Sheets, labels, frontmatter, review queues.

Related concepts

Review, maturity, false authority, AI-assisted content, high-risk content, source fidelity, field-informed learning.

Risks / failure modes

  • Labels become stale.
  • Labels create false hierarchy.
  • Review bottlenecks form around a few people.
  • High-risk content is labeled but not handled differently.
  • Review becomes gatekeeping rather than stewardship.

Consent / privacy notes

Community profiles, people maps, exact locations, sensitive local knowledge, and AI-assisted recommendations require privacy and consent review before public use.

Power / control notes

Review power can become hidden governance. Reviewer roles, criteria, and appeal/correction paths should be visible.

Regenerative obligation notes

Review protects contributors, source communities, affected communities, ecosystems, and future users from misuse or overclaiming.

Deployment requirements

Define maturity states, review types, high-risk triggers, reviewer roles, and review cadence.

Maturity status

Draft / Priority option

Open questions

  • Which sections require review before public release?
  • Who can assign or change maturity labels?
  • How should review labor be recognized?

2. Resource Graph and Ecosystem Mapping Options

These options help a community map the field around it without collapsing into a link dump.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Resource Graph

Structured registry of entities, resources, tools, source systems, and relationships.

Google Sheets, Airtable, Notion, CSV, GitHub, graph databases

Becomes a stale spreadsheet or overbuilt graph.

Draft

Ecosystem Atlas

Multi-view interpretive map of actors, tools, concepts, places, and relationships.

Kumu, Figma, Miro, graph databases

Too broad; visually impressive but hard to use.

Candidate

Source system registry

List of living knowledge systems to credit and revisit.

Wikis, forums, maps, GitHub repos

Treats source systems as extractable link pools.

Candidate

Actor map

Map of people, orgs, communities, and networks.

Kumu, Graph Commons, spreadsheets

Consent and representation risks.

High-risk candidate

Tool map

Map of tools by function and interoperability.

Resource Graph, GitHub, docs

Tool comparison without context.

Candidate

Concept map

Visual map of ideas and relationships.

Kumu, Loopy, Miro, FigJam

Beautiful but vague.

Candidate

Place / bioregion map

Map of local nodes, watersheds, communities, projects, and ecological contexts.

Hylo, QGIS, OpenStreetMap, Restor

Exposes sensitive locations or communities.

High-risk candidate

Event harvest map

Captures resources and connections from gatherings.

Event notes, recordings, docs

Outputs disappear after event.

Candidate

Relationship grammar

Shared vocabulary for relationships between entries.

Ontology docs, graph schemas

Too many relationship types.

Candidate

Function tagging

Tags resources by what they help do.

publish, map, fund, verify, coordinate, sensemake, discover

Tags become inconsistent.

Candidate

To-place inbox

Holding area for resources not yet classified.

Docs, spreadsheets, GitHub issues

Becomes permanent junk drawer.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Resource Graph

Short description

A structured registry that classifies resources, tools, organizations, source systems, cases, datasets, frameworks, and other entities by type, function, source lineage, review status, and relationships.

Problem addressed

Regenerative web3, public goods, knowledge commons, local coordination, and ecological stewardship resources are scattered across docs, forums, maps, repos, websites, and community memory. Without structure, the Toolkit becomes a link dump.

Useful when

  • A group needs to map what exists across a complex ecosystem.
  • Resources need source lineage, type, function, and review status.
  • Entries must route into Encyclopedia, Option Library, Implementation Memory, Deployment, or Open Questions.
  • Contributors need a practical table they can add to without understanding the whole architecture.

Not useful when

  • The group has too few resources to justify structure.
  • The graph becomes overengineered before a simple table is useful.
  • Relationships are added without enough clarity to support navigation or action.

Required roles

Resource gardener, source lineage steward, reviewer, knowledge steward.

Required infrastructure

Structured table or database; type, domain, function, source lineage, status, and notes fields; optional relationship fields; contributor workflow.

Related tools

Google Sheets, Airtable, Notion, GitHub, CSV, Kumu, Neo4j, RDF, Geo-like knowledge spaces.

Related concepts

Ontology, Resource Graph, Ecosystem Atlas, source system, source lineage, relationship grammar, review and maturity.

Risks / failure modes

  • Link dump with metadata.
  • Stale spreadsheet.
  • Duplicate entries.
  • Too many categories.
  • Source systems treated as extractable resources.
  • Sensitive people, places, or communities mapped without consent.
  • Graph theater: impressive maps that do not help action.

Consent / privacy notes

Actor maps, people profiles, community profiles, local maps, and exact locations require consent and visibility rules.

Power / control notes

Whoever controls the Resource Graph can shape discoverability, legitimacy, and attention. Contribution and correction paths should be visible.

Regenerative obligation notes

The graph should credit source systems, avoid extraction, and route value or attention back where possible.

Deployment requirements

Start with a minimum viable table before moving to graph databases or complex infrastructure.

Maturity status

Draft / Priority option

Open questions

  • What are the minimum required fields?
  • Which relationship types are useful enough for v0.1?
  • Which resources should be structured first?

3. Governance and Coordination Options

These options help groups make decisions, distribute roles, coordinate work, handle tensions, and surface adverse signals.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Working group model

Small groups responsible for specific domains.

DAO working groups, guilds, circles

Fragmentation, unclear authority.

Candidate

Council model

Smaller body holds decision or stewardship role.

Councils, multisigs, committees

Capture, legitimacy gaps.

High-risk candidate

Consent-based decision-making

Decisions proceed unless there is a reasoned objection.

Sociocracy, Loomio

Requires facilitation skill.

Candidate

Proposal flow

Structured process for submitting, reviewing, and deciding proposals.

Forums, Charmverse, Snapshot, Gardens, GitHub

Process theater, bottlenecks.

Candidate

Delegated review

Trusted reviewers assess resources, proposals, or content.

Review committees, curators

Reviewer capture or fatigue.

Candidate

Role-based stewardship

Roles define obligations, not only permissions.

Hats Protocol, Holacracy, Sociocracy

Role inflation, unclear accountability.

Candidate

Multisig execution

Shared wallet controlled by multiple signers.

Safe, multisigs

Hidden power, signer inactivity.

High-risk candidate

Dispute or objection path

Process for contesting decisions or representations.

Governance forums, arbitration, mediation

Underused if socially unsafe.

Candidate

Conflict transformation pathway

Structured way to work with conflict as signal.

Mediation, restorative practices

Requires skilled facilitation.

Candidate

Decision log

Record of what was decided, by whom, and why.

GitHub, docs, forums

Not maintained.

Candidate

Stewardship rotation

Rotating roles to avoid centralization and burnout.

Role cycles, councils

Loss of continuity.

Candidate

Adverse signal channel

Place where harms, risks, or failures can be surfaced.

Forms, ombuds, trusted stewards

No named response.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Adverse Signal Channel

Short description

A designated pathway for surfacing harms, risks, failures, objections, weak signals, or structural concerns, paired with a named response process.

Problem addressed

Many coordination systems collect feedback but do not define where adverse signals go, who sees them, who responds, or what happens next. Detection without named response allows risks to accumulate.

Useful when

  • A deployment affects funds, people, communities, ecological claims, public maps, AI recommendations, governance decisions, or local contexts.
  • Participants need a safe way to surface concerns.
  • Structural integrity review identifies unresolved exposure.

Not useful when

  • The channel exists only symbolically.
  • No one is responsible for response.
  • Social conditions make reporting unsafe.

Required roles

Response steward, reviewer, facilitator or mediator where needed, deployment steward.

Required infrastructure

Reporting form, private channel, trusted steward, ombuds path, issue tracker, or governance forum; named response process; escalation and privacy rules; unresolved issue log.

Related tools

Forms, secure email, Signal, private channels, GitHub issues, Charmverse, forums, ombuds processes, mediation practices.

Related concepts

Structural integrity, adverse signal, named response, information asymmetry, power/control, regenerative obligation, conflict transformation.

Risks / failure modes

  • No named response.
  • Retaliation or social risk.
  • Concerns disappear into private channels.
  • Only loud or high-status participants are heard.
  • Channel becomes a complaint sink instead of a learning path.
  • Sensitive information is exposed.

Consent / privacy notes

Reports may contain sensitive personal, community, financial, ecological, or conflict-related information. Visibility levels must be defined.

Power / control notes

The party receiving adverse signals should not be the only party with power to interpret or dismiss them.

Regenerative obligation notes

A commons must create pathways for correction, contestation, and repair, especially for people or communities affected by representation, funding, mapping, or implementation decisions.

Deployment requirements

Every high-risk deployment should identify what adverse signals are expected, where they go, who responds, and what response options exist.

Maturity status

Candidate / Priority option

Open questions

  • Who can safely receive adverse signals?
  • What signals require pause or redesign?
  • How should unresolved signals update the Toolkit?

4. Funding, Allocation, and Resource Flow Options

These options help communities allocate money, grants, matching funds, rewards, local vouchers, mutual credit, and other resources.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Direct grants

Funds allocated directly by a person or committee.

Gitcoin, foundations, councils

Centralization, bias.

High-risk candidate

Quadratic funding

Matching funds amplify broad support.

Gitcoin, Allo, QF paper

Sybil attacks, popularity bias.

High-risk candidate

Retroactive funding

Rewards work after impact is observed.

Optimism Retro Funding, Hypercerts

Hard to assess impact fairly.

High-risk candidate

Milestone-based funding

Payments tied to progress milestones.

Karma GAP, grant agreements

Over-administration, milestone gaming.

Draft

Conviction voting

Preference accumulates over time.

Commons Stack, Gardens-like systems

Capture by patient capital or insiders.

High-risk candidate

Participatory budgeting

Community allocates a budget collectively.

Decidim, civic PB systems

Participation fatigue.

Candidate

Matching pool

Funds match donations, votes, or other signals.

Gitcoin, Allo

Gaming and eligibility disputes.

High-risk candidate

Streaming payments

Continuous funds flow over time.

Superfluid

Harder to stop or review.

Candidate

Donation pool

Shared pool for donors to support projects.

Giveth, Open Collective

Weak accountability if not designed.

Candidate

Local vouchers

Locally redeemable promises for goods or services.

Sarafu-like systems, Citizen Wallet

Legal/tax complexity, liquidity issues.

High-risk candidate

Community currency

Local exchange medium or mutual credit system.

Grassroots Economics, Circles, GoodDollar

Trust, compliance, adoption burden.

High-risk candidate

Impact attestations

Structured records of claims or contributions.

EAS, Hypercerts, Green Goods

Overclaiming, weak evidence.

High-risk candidate

Public goods reporting

Transparent updates on use of funds and progress.

Karma GAP, OpenGrants, OSO

Reporting burden, metric theater.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Milestone-Based Funding

Short description

A funding pattern where disbursement, review, or accountability is tied to defined project milestones, deliverables, or progress updates.

Problem addressed

Public goods and regenerative projects often receive funds without clear progress structure, reporting cadence, evidence expectations, or learning loops. This can create weak accountability, funder uncertainty, and poor implementation memory.

Useful when

  • Projects can define meaningful milestones without over-bureaucratizing the work.
  • Funders need progress visibility.
  • Project teams need staged support.
  • Milestones are used for learning and coordination, not only compliance.

Not useful when

  • Work is exploratory and cannot be meaningfully divided into milestones.
  • Milestones incentivize performative deliverables over real learning or impact.
  • Small grassroots groups lack reporting capacity.
  • Funders use milestones to impose extractive oversight.

Required roles

Project team, funding steward, reviewer, milestone approver, optional community reviewer.

Required infrastructure

Project profile, milestone template, reporting channel, evidence submission process, review process, funding disbursement process.

Related tools

Karma GAP, Charmverse, GitHub, Notion, Google Drive, OpenGrants, public reports, multisig processes, EAS, Hypercerts.

Related concepts

Public goods funding, accountability, stewardship obligation, source lineage, evidence quality, signal vs conclusion, regenerative obligation.

Risks / failure modes

  • Milestone gaming.
  • Metric theater.
  • Administrative overload.
  • Reporting replaces implementation.
  • Weak signals treated as conclusions.
  • Funders ignore qualitative context.
  • Reporting requirements exclude smaller or less formal groups.

Consent / privacy notes

Milestone reports may include people, places, community activities, funding details, or ecological sites. Visibility and consent should be defined.

Power / control notes

Milestone approval gives reviewers or funders significant power. Criteria, appeal paths, and disbursement control should be visible.

Regenerative obligation notes

Funded projects owe some form of reporting to funders, collaborators, affected communities, and future learners, but reporting should not become extractive bureaucracy.

Deployment requirements

Before use, define milestone criteria, review roles, evidence standards, payment triggers, appeal path, and reporting burden limits.

Maturity status

Draft / Field-informed

Open questions

  • What kinds of milestones are appropriate for local regenerative work?
  • How can reporting remain lightweight and useful?
  • When should milestone funding be avoided?

5. Discovery, Affinity, and Participation Options

These options help people find topics, resources, communities, collaborators, requests, offers, and contribution pathways.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Role-based entry points

Navigation by user role or intention.

Docs, websites, onboarding flows

Over-segmentation.

Candidate

Interest profile

Person or group indicates interests.

Forms, profiles, Hylo-like systems

Privacy and inference risk.

High-risk candidate

Skill map

Map of skills people offer.

Directories, profiles, community platforms

Outdated, status games.

High-risk candidate

Needs / offers board

Public or community board of requests and offers.

Hylo, forums, Airtable, Notion

Noise, unclear fulfillment.

Candidate

Local discovery map

Map of nearby communities or projects.

Hylo Map, OpenStreetMap, QGIS

Location sensitivity.

High-risk candidate

Opt-in matchable profile

Profile can be used for recommendations or matching.

Consent-based systems

Consent ambiguity.

High-risk candidate

Contributor pathway

Steps for moving from interest to contribution.

Onboarding docs, bounties, GitHub issues

Too rigid or gamified.

Candidate

Learning path

Curated sequence for a topic or role.

Docs, courses, playlists

Becomes outdated.

Candidate

Opportunity board

List of tasks, grants, events, roles, or calls.

Charmverse, GitHub issues, Dework-like tools

Maintenance burden.

Candidate

Mentor / peer matching

Matches people for learning or support.

Forms, community platforms

Emotional labor, privacy.

High-risk candidate

AI-assisted recommendation with consent

Uses AI to suggest resources or connections under explicit consent rules.

RAG, embeddings, recommender systems

Surveillance, bias, false matches.

High-risk candidate

Priority Entry: Opt-In Discovery Profile

Short description

A consent-based profile that allows a person, group, community, project, or local node to be discoverable under defined visibility, contact, matching, and data-use conditions.

Problem addressed

Communities need ways to find relevant people, projects, skills, needs, offers, and local nodes. But discovery systems can easily become surveillance systems if visibility, contact, location, identity, and AI use are not consent-based.

Useful when

  • People or communities want to be discoverable.
  • Discovery requires more than a public directory.
  • Matching depends on interests, skills, needs, offers, location, or contribution history.
  • The system can support clear consent and correction paths.

Not useful when

  • Consent is vague or one-time.
  • Users cannot control visibility.
  • Profiles expose sensitive community or location information.
  • AI recommendations use profile data without explicit permission.

Required roles

Profile owner, discovery steward, privacy reviewer, technical maintainer, community reviewer where communities are represented.

Required infrastructure

Profile form, visibility settings, consent fields, contact preference fields, correction or withdrawal process, optional matching engine or directory view.

Related tools

Hylo, forms, Airtable, Notion, community directories, Solid-like data systems, decentralized identity tools, consent-based matching tools.

Related concepts

Discovery and affinity, privacy, consent, identity, local coordination, community representation, data minimization, regenerative obligation.

Risks / failure modes

  • Public visibility without real consent.
  • Outdated profiles.
  • Unwanted contact.
  • Location exposure.
  • AI inference beyond consent.
  • Social graph exposure.
  • Contributor histories used as reputation without context.

Consent / privacy notes

Consent should be granular: listed, described, contacted, recommended, matched, location used, contribution history used, AI-assisted routing used.

Power / control notes

The profile owner should control visibility and updates. The system should not force discoverability as a condition of participation.

Regenerative obligation notes

Discovery should create useful connection without extracting identity, availability, social networks, or local knowledge.

Deployment requirements

Define profile fields, visibility levels, consent categories, location precision, update cadence, correction path, and AI-use restrictions.

Maturity status

Candidate / High-risk / Priority option

Open questions

  • What is the minimum useful profile?
  • How should local/community profiles differ from individual profiles?
  • What should never be used for AI-assisted matching?

6. Impact, Evidence, and MRV Options

These options help communities represent claims, evidence, monitoring, outcomes, verification, and learning.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Claim-evidence record

Separates claims from supporting evidence.

EAS, Hypercerts, docs, databases

Evidence quality varies.

Candidate

Impact report

Narrative and data report of outcomes.

Karma GAP, OpenGrants, public docs

Marketing over learning.

Candidate

MRV workflow

Monitoring, reporting, and verification process.

OpenMRV, Verra, Plan Vivo, Gold Standard

High burden, false precision.

High-risk candidate

Community science observation

Local observations contribute evidence.

iNaturalist, GBIF, farmOS

Data quality and consent issues.

High-risk candidate

Field record system

Structured logs from ecological or local work.

farmOS, spreadsheets, mobile forms

Maintenance burden.

Candidate

Attested contribution

Onchain or signed record of contribution.

EAS, Hypercerts

Privacy, overclaiming.

High-risk candidate

Biodiversity evidence checklist

Checklist for biodiversity-related claims.

GBIF, iNaturalist, ecological review

Incomplete indicators.

High-risk candidate

Restoration monitoring map

Map and monitor restoration sites.

Restor, Global Forest Watch, QGIS

Remote sensing mismatch.

High-risk candidate

Failure / adverse outcome report

Records what did not work or caused harm.

Retrospectives, incident reports

Reputational fear.

Candidate

Evidence quality label

Marks confidence or review level.

Maturity badges, review workflows

Can appear more precise than it is.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Claim-Evidence Record

Short description

A structured record that separates a claim from the evidence, signals, sources, uncertainty, and review status supporting or challenging it.

Problem addressed

Impact reports, grant updates, ecological claims, and public goods narratives often blur the difference between activity, output, outcome, impact, signal, evidence, and conclusion.

Useful when

  • A project makes public claims about impact, progress, ecological outcomes, governance outcomes, or funding use.
  • Attestations, hypercerts, reports, or dashboards are used.
  • Reviewers need to distinguish evidence from interpretation.
  • The Toolkit needs to avoid treating detection as proof.

Not useful when

  • The claim is low-stakes and informal.
  • Evidence standards are too heavy for early learning notes.
  • The record creates false precision around complex social or ecological outcomes.

Required roles

Claim author, evidence curator, reviewer, domain reviewer where needed, community or ecological reviewer for high-risk claims.

Required infrastructure
