Claim field, evidence field, source lineage field, evidence type field, review status field, uncertainty field, correction path.

Related tools

EAS, Hypercerts, Karma GAP, OpenGrants, GitHub, Google Docs, databases, dashboards, spreadsheets.

Related concepts

Signal vs conclusion, evidence quality, source lineage, MRV, impact integrity, regenerative obligation, overclaiming.

Risks / failure modes

  • Weak evidence presented as strong evidence.
  • Activity metrics treated as impact.
  • Onchain records treated as truth.
  • Ecological claims made without ecological review.
  • Community experience contradicts public reporting.
  • Evidence is cherry-picked.

Consent / privacy notes

Evidence may include photos, locations, personal data, community stories, field observations, or ecological site data. Visibility and reuse permissions are required.

Power / control notes

The party making the claim should not be the only party assessing its evidence quality.

Regenerative obligation notes

Claims should be proportional, contextual, correctable, and accountable to affected communities and ecosystems.

Deployment requirements

Define claim categories, evidence types, review requirements, uncertainty labels, and correction path.

Maturity status

Candidate / Priority option

Open questions

  • What is the minimum viable claim-evidence template?
  • Which claims require ecological, legal, community, or structural review?
  • How should uncertainty be represented without weakening useful learning?

7. AI and Sensemaking Options

These options support curation, interpretation, deliberation, classification, search, and adaptive learning.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

AI-assisted classification

AI suggests tags, types, or relationships.

LLMs, embeddings, RAG

Semantic drift, hallucination.

High-risk candidate

AI-assisted summary

AI summarizes notes, sources, or events.

LLMs

Source loss, flattening.

High-risk candidate

Human-in-the-loop review

Human review required before publication.

GitHub PRs, review queues

Bottleneck.

Candidate

Semantic search

Search by meaning, not only keywords.

Embeddings, vector databases

Poor explainability.

Candidate

RAG knowledge assistant

AI answers from curated sources.

RAG pipelines

Overconfidence, retrieval gaps.

High-risk candidate

Deliberation synthesis

Summarizes group discussion and disagreement.

Harmonica, Pol.is, Kialo

False consensus.

High-risk candidate

Signal clustering

Groups repeated issues, questions, or feedback.

AI tools, spreadsheets, sensemaking tools

Misclassification.

Candidate

Unresolved tension log

Keeps tensions visible rather than forcing closure.

Docs, issue trackers

Becomes ignored.

Candidate

Pattern extraction

Turns repeated observations into candidate patterns.

AI + human synthesis

Overgeneralization.

High-risk candidate

Model use disclosure

Labels AI-assisted content or recommendations.

Metadata fields

Not consistently applied.

Candidate

Priority Entry: AI-Assisted Draft with Human Review

Short description

A workflow where AI supports drafting, summarizing, classifying, or synthesizing, but human stewards review source fidelity, context, uncertainty, and publication readiness before content enters the commons as knowledge.

Problem addressed

AI can accelerate knowledge gardening but can also create false authority, hallucinated connections, source loss, flattened context, and overconfident synthesis.

Useful when

  • The source material is large, fragmented, or difficult to organize manually.
  • AI output is treated as draft, not knowledge.
  • Source lineage and human review are required.
  • The workflow has clear labels for AI-assisted content.

Not useful when

  • No human reviewer is available.
  • Sources are sensitive, private, or consent-limited.
  • The output will influence high-risk deployment without review.
  • The task requires strong factual precision without source verification.

Required roles

AI operator, human reviewer, source lineage steward, domain reviewer where needed.

Required infrastructure

AI-use label, source list, review checklist, version history, correction path.

Related tools

LLMs, RAG workflows, semantic search, GitHub review, Google Docs suggestions, knowledge graph tools.

Related concepts

AI-assisted curation, false authority, source fidelity, human-in-the-loop review, semantic drift, synthesis, review and maturity.

Risks / failure modes

  • Hallucinated facts or relationships.
  • Lost source context.
  • Flattened community nuance.
  • Polished draft mistaken for reviewed knowledge.
  • AI recommendations violate consent.
  • Review labor becomes invisible.

Consent / privacy notes

Do not use sensitive people, community, location, identity, contribution history, or private governance data in AI workflows without explicit permission.

Power / control notes

AI workflows can centralize interpretation in whoever prompts, selects, and edits outputs. Review and source visibility are necessary.

Regenerative obligation notes

AI should support human knowledge stewardship, not erase contributors, sources, local context, or dissent.

Deployment requirements

Define AI-use labels, what content AI can process, who reviews outputs, what cannot be published without review, and how corrections are handled.

Maturity status

Candidate / High-risk

Open questions

  • Which AI uses should be prohibited or require stronger review?
  • How should AI-assisted drafts be labeled in the public Toolkit?
  • What is the minimum source fidelity checklist?

8. Infrastructure and Publishing Options

These options support the technical substrate of the Toolkit.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Single shared workspace

One working workspace for collaboration.

Google Docs, Notion

Can become too large and entropic.

Field-informed / Entropy risk

Modular document set

Separates architecture, ontology, option library, resource registry, and working notes.

Google Docs tabs, Markdown, GitHub, Notion

Fragmentation if navigation is weak.

Draft

Markdown repository

Source-controlled knowledge files.

GitHub, Git, Markdown

Contributor barrier.

Candidate

Static docs site

Public browsable version.

Astro, Starlight, Quartz

Publishing does not equal maintaining.

Candidate

Spreadsheet resource graph

Tabular registry of resources.

Google Sheets, Airtable

Weak relationship modeling.

Candidate

Graph database

Structured relationship database.

Neo4j, RDF, property graphs

Overengineering.

Candidate

Governed knowledge space

Community-curated graph or knowledge space.

Geo-like systems

Governance complexity.

Candidate

Ingestion pipeline

Moves resources into structured records.

KOI-like systems, scripts, AI workflows

Premature automation.

Candidate

Portable frontmatter

Metadata stored in text files.

Markdown frontmatter

Metadata drift.

Candidate

Archive export

Periodic export for preservation.

ZIP, Git, CSV, JSON

Forgotten if not automated.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Modular Document Set

Short description

A publishing and stewardship pattern that decomposes a large working document into separate but connected artifacts.

Problem addressed

A single large living document can become difficult to read, maintain, review, or use. Sections accumulate without clear stewardship, and contributors struggle to tell what is normative, descriptive, or provisional.

Useful when

  • The document is too large for most contributors to read end-to-end.
  • Different sections need different review cadences.
  • Registries, architecture, ontology, and working notes are mixed together.
  • The team needs clearer contribution paths.

Not useful when

  • The project is still too early to justify multiple artifacts.
  • Fragmentation would make navigation worse.
  • There is no steward responsible for keeping modules linked.

Required roles

Knowledge steward, document maintainer, ontology steward, option librarian, resource graph steward.

Required infrastructure

Clear navigation, stable filenames/tabs, cross-links, changelog, contribution guide, and status labels.

Related tools

Google Docs tabs, GitHub, Markdown, Notion, Obsidian, static docs sites.

Related concepts

Entropy management, normative vs descriptive vs provisional content, minimum ontology, review and maturity, knowledge stewardship.

Risks / failure modes

  • Modules drift apart.
  • Contributors do not know where to add content.
  • Working notes still become hidden governance.
  • Splitting becomes busywork instead of stewardship.
  • The architecture becomes harder, not easier, to navigate.

Deployment requirements

Start with five artifacts: Architecture Specification, Minimum Ontology, Option Library, Resource Registry, and Working Notes. Keep a clear index that shows how they connect.

Maturity status

Draft / Priority option

Open questions

  • Which sections should be split first?
  • What remains in the master document?
  • Who maintains cross-links and version history?

9. Legal, Institutional, and Stewardship Options

These options help projects interface with real-world institutions and obligations.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Fiscal sponsorship

Existing entity receives and manages funds.

Open Collective, foundations

Sponsor dependency.

High-risk candidate

Nonprofit entity

Mission-driven legal structure.

Jurisdiction-specific

Administrative burden.

High-risk candidate

Cooperative entity

Member-owned structure.

Platform cooperatives

Governance complexity.

High-risk candidate

Steward ownership

Ownership designed around mission stewardship.

Purpose Foundation

Legal complexity.

High-risk candidate

DAO legal wrapper

Legal form around DAO operations.

LexDAO, DAO legal research

Jurisdiction risk.

High-risk candidate

Open license

Defines reuse rights for knowledge.

Creative Commons

Wrong license choice.

Candidate

Contributor agreement

Defines contribution rights and expectations.

Open-source practices

Too formal for early stage.

Candidate

Data trust / stewardship model

Structure for managing sensitive data.

Data governance work

High complexity.

High-risk candidate

Public-sector partnership

Collaboration with municipal or public institutions.

Civic tech examples

Capture or bureaucracy.

High-risk candidate

Policy template

Reusable policy draft for organizations.

Crypto Altruists nonprofit policy resources

Requires legal review.

High-risk candidate

Priority Entry: Open License

Short description

A clearly stated reuse license for Toolkit content, resources, templates, or published materials.

Problem addressed

Knowledge commons need reuse rights to be legible. Without licensing clarity, contributors and users may not know whether content can be copied, adapted, translated, remixed, or published elsewhere.

Useful when

  • The Toolkit publishes public material.
  • Templates or guides are meant to be reused.
  • Contributors need clarity about how their work can circulate.
  • Source systems with their own licenses are referenced or adapted.

Not useful when

  • Content includes sensitive, private, consent-limited, or community-restricted knowledge.
  • The Toolkit has not clarified what it owns, adapts, or merely references.
  • Legal review is needed but unavailable.

Required roles

Knowledge steward, legal/institutional reviewer where needed, contributor steward.

Required infrastructure

License notice, contribution expectations, source lineage fields, and restrictions for content that is not openly reusable.

Related tools

Creative Commons licenses, open-source licenses, contributor agreements, source lineage metadata.

Related concepts

Open knowledge, commons stewardship, attribution, source lineage, consent, non-extractive reuse.

Risks / failure modes

  • Applying an open license to content the Toolkit does not own.
  • Reusing source-system material outside its license or community norms.
  • Treating open licensing as permission to extract.
  • Choosing a license that blocks intended reuse.
  • Publishing sensitive community knowledge under an overly permissive license.

Deployment requirements

Clarify which parts of the Toolkit are original, adapted, cited, private, public, or restricted. Do not apply one license blindly to all material.

Maturity status

Candidate / Requires legal review

Open questions

  • What license fits the Toolkit’s public materials?
  • How should adapted source-system material be handled?
  • What content should never be openly licensed?

10. Culture, Care, and Social Practice Options

These options support the relational infrastructure of the commons.

Key Options

Option

Short description

Example tools / references

Key risks

Status

Onboarding ritual

Welcomes people into shared context.

Community calls, docs, workshops

Performative if not supported.

Candidate

Community call rhythm

Regular gathering for alignment and updates.

Zoom, Discord, Hylo, forums

Meeting fatigue.

Candidate

Recognition practice

Names and appreciates contributions.

POAPs, shoutouts, attestations

Status games.

Candidate

Contributor care check

Regular check on capacity and burnout.

Surveys, retrospectives

Ignored if inconvenient.

Candidate

Role offboarding

Clear way to leave or transfer roles.

Role docs, handoff templates

Often forgotten.

Candidate

Retrospective ritual

Structured reflection after work.

After-action reviews

Turns into vague discussion.

Candidate

Repair pathway

Process for addressing harm or conflict.

Mediation, restorative practice

Needs trust and skill.

High-risk candidate

Movement glossary

Shared language and translation layer.

Docs, glossary pages

Insider jargon.

Candidate

Story archive

Preserves narratives, origin stories, and lessons.

Blogs, podcasts, videos

Mythologizes instead of learns.

Candidate

Seasonal planning rhythm

Periodic planning and reflection cycle.

Quarterly cycles, sprints

Can become bureaucratic.

Candidate

Priority Entry: Implementation Retrospective

Short description

A structured reflection after a deployment, project, event, campaign, funding round, sprint, or experiment that captures what happened, what worked, what failed, what signals emerged, and what should update the Toolkit.

Problem addressed

Projects often produce outputs but lose the learning. Without retrospectives, the Toolkit becomes theoretical and communities repeat the same failures.

Useful when

  • A deployment has completed a phase.
  • A funding round, event, sprint, local campaign, or tool experiment has generated signals.
  • Something failed or created friction.
  • The Toolkit needs to harvest patterns without overgeneralizing.

Not useful when

  • Participants are too exhausted and the process would become extractive.
  • The retrospective has no route into Implementation Memory or Evolution.
  • The group cannot create enough safety to discuss failure honestly.

Required roles

Facilitator, participants, note-taker, implementation memory steward, optional reviewer.

Required infrastructure

Retrospective template, safe discussion space, notes or recording rules, source lineage and consent notes, routing path into Implementation Memory and Evolution.

Related tools

Google Docs, Miro, FigJam, Charmverse, GitHub issues, forms, meeting notes, implementation case templates.

Related concepts

Implementation Memory, Evolution, pattern harvest, unresolved tension, contributor care, regenerative obligation, source lineage.

Risks / failure modes

  • Vague discussion with no usable outputs.
  • Failure is hidden due to reputational fear.
  • Hidden labor is not named.
  • Lessons are captured but never routed into the Toolkit.
  • Participants are asked to reflect without care or reciprocity.

Consent / privacy notes

Retrospectives may include conflict, failure, private community context, financial issues, or sensitive local information. Decide what can be public, anonymized, internal, or omitted.

Power / control notes

Facilitators and editors shape the memory of what happened. Participants should be able to correct or contest summaries.

Regenerative obligation notes

Implementation learning should credit participants, protect sensitive context, and support future users without extracting from the people who did the work.

Deployment requirements

Define when retrospectives happen, who participates, where outputs go, how privacy is handled, and what updates the Toolkit.

Maturity status

Draft / Priority option

Open questions

  • What is the minimum useful retrospective?
  • How should failed experiments be represented without blame?
  • How does a retrospective become a pattern harvest?

Option-to-Tool Map

This map preserves useful tool references without confusing tools for options.

Option

Possible tools / references

Public knowledge base

Google Docs, Notion, GitHub, Astro, Starlight, Quartz

Digital garden

Obsidian, Quartz, Logseq, Foam, SuperBenefit Knowledge Garden

Resource Graph

Google Sheets, Airtable, Notion, CSV, GitHub, graph databases

Ecosystem Atlas

Kumu, Miro, FigJam, graph databases, spreadsheets

Source Lineage Field

W3C PROV, Dublin Core, Markdown frontmatter, Zotero

Proposal Flow

Charmverse, forums, GitHub Issues, Snapshot, Gardens-like systems

Participatory Deliberation

Pol.is, Decidim, Loomio, vTaiwan, Kialo

Public Goods Funding

Gitcoin, Allo, Octant, Giveth, Open Collective

Milestone-Based Funding

Karma GAP, grant agreements, public reports

Public Goods Reporting

Karma GAP, OpenGrants, Open Source Observer, public docs

Impact Attestation

Hypercerts, EAS, Green Goods

Claim-Evidence Record

EAS, Hypercerts, docs, databases, dashboards

Ecological Monitoring

farmOS, Restor, iNaturalist, GBIF, Global Forest Watch, QGIS

Local Discovery Map

Hylo, OpenStreetMap, QGIS, opt-in directories

AI Sensemaking

Harmonica, Bonfires, RAG workflows, semantic search, Pol.is

Privacy-Sensitive Coordination

Signal, Nym, OpenMined-inspired patterns, selective visibility settings

Community Currency

Sarafu, Citizen Wallet, Circles, GoodDollar

Open Licensing

Creative Commons

Fiscal Sponsorship

Open Collective, foundations, jurisdiction-specific fiscal sponsors

Structural Review Notes for Options

Every full option entry should eventually be reviewed through the following lenses.

1. Conditions

What must be true for this option to work?

Examples:

  • sufficient shared context;
  • clear participants;
  • stable source of truth;
  • review capacity;
  • facilitation capacity;
  • technical maintenance;
  • legal understanding;
  • community legitimacy;
  • evidence standards;
  • privacy boundaries.

2. Dependencies

What does the option depend on?

Examples:

  • a tool;
  • a role;
  • a funder;
  • a dataset;
  • a protocol;
  • a shared process;
  • a social norm;
  • a legal entity;
  • an API;
  • a review committee;
  • a local steward.

3. Failure Modes

What can go wrong?

Examples:

  • capture;
  • gaming;
  • burnout;
  • overcomplexity;
  • underuse;
  • poor evidence;
  • weak consent;
  • maintenance decay;
  • hidden power;
  • false legitimacy;
  • platform lock-in;
  • financialization;
  • metric theater;
  • social exclusion.

4. Power and Control

Who controls:

  • funds;
  • infrastructure;
  • admin access;
  • metadata;
  • moderation;
  • review;
  • publication;
  • identity;
  • treasury;
  • decision paths;
  • data exports;
  • public narrative?

5. Consent and Privacy

Does this option involve:

  • people profiles;
  • locations;
  • community representation;
  • contribution histories;
  • sensitive local knowledge;
  • contact preferences;
  • identity data;
  • recommendation systems;
  • AI-assisted matching;
  • ecological site data?

6. Regenerative Obligation

Who should be credited, consulted, protected, compensated, or given a correction path?

Examples:

  • source creators;
  • local communities;
  • knowledge holders;
  • contributors;
  • reviewers;
  • maintainers;
  • affected ecosystems;
  • future users;
  • implementation participants.

7. Evidence Quality

What supports this option?

Examples:

  • theory only;
  • source-linked reference;
  • implementation case;
  • repeated use;
  • field-informed learning;
  • reviewed pattern;
  • mixed evidence;
  • contested evidence;
  • high-risk or weak evidence.

Option Library Governance Rules

Rule 1: Every option must have the primary ontology type “Option”

Tools, resources, source systems, concepts, deployments, and cases should be linked inside option entries, not treated as standalone options.

Rule 2: Options must be reusable

A one-off deployment is not an option. A specific case may be included as an example, but the option should describe a pathway that others could adapt.

Rule 3: Options must name conditions

An option should make clear when it is useful and when it is not useful.

Rule 4: Options must name risks and failure modes

An option is incomplete if it only describes benefits.

Rule 5: Options must distinguish tools from design choices

Karma GAP is a tool. Milestone-Based Funding or Public Goods Reporting are options.

EAS is a protocol. Impact Attestation or Claim-Evidence Record are options.

Hylo is a tool. Local Discovery Map or Needs / Offers Board are options.

Rule 6: Options must distinguish signals from conclusions

An option may identify useful signals, but signals do not automatically prove impact, success, or suitability.

Rule 7: High-risk options require stronger review before deployment

High-risk options include those involving:

  • funds or resource allocation;
  • ecological, carbon, biodiversity, or restoration claims;
  • people or community profiles;
  • local maps or exact locations;
  • AI-assisted recommendations;
  • governance / coordination recommendations;
  • legal, tax, or institutional implications;
  • identity, reputation, or contribution histories;
  • public claims about impact.

Rule 8: Options should evolve through implementation memory

When an option is used in practice, implementation learning should update the option’s risks, conditions, examples, evidence quality, or maturity status.

Priority Option Entries to Develop Next

The current v0.1 includes full or partial priority entries for:

  1. Source Lineage Field
  2. Review and Maturity System
  3. Resource Graph
  4. Adverse Signal Channel
  5. Milestone-Based Funding
  6. Opt-In Discovery Profile
  7. Claim-Evidence Record
  8. AI-Assisted Draft with Human Review
  9. Modular Document Set
  10. Open License
  11. Implementation Retrospective

Next full entries recommended:

  1. Ecosystem Atlas
  2. Relationship Grammar
  3. Structural Exposure Card
  4. Public Goods Reporting
  5. Impact Attestations
  6. Local Discovery Map
  7. Contribution Guide
  8. Open-Question Page
  9. Pattern Harvest
  10. Consent Checklist
  11. Evidence Quality Label

Concept Preservation Note

This updated Option Library does not attempt to preserve every old phrase or every old table row.

It does preserve the conceptual range of the previous Option Library by carrying forward the main option families:

  • knowledge commons;
  • resource graph and ecosystem mapping;
  • governance and coordination;
  • funding and allocation;
  • discovery, affinity, and participation;
  • impact, evidence, and MRV;
  • AI and sensemaking;
  • infrastructure and publishing;
  • legal, institutional, and stewardship choices;
  • culture, care, and social practice.

Specific tools, projects, source systems, and references should be routed to the Resource Graph, Source System Registry, Concept & Idea Ecology, or Implementation Memory rather than treated as options.

The intent is not deletion. The intent is classification.

Migration Guidance

When replacing the old Option Library, use this rule:

Previous entry type

New destination

Reusable design choice

Keep in Option Library

Specific tool or platform

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

Source system

Source System Registry

Concept or framework

Encyclopedia / Concept & Idea Ecology

Specific case or project

Implementation Memory

Deployment checklist or operational process

Deployment & Structural Integrity

Evidence from practice

Implementation Memory / Evolution

Unresolved issue

Open Questions / Research Backlog

Failure example

Failure Case Library / Anti-Pattern Library

Deprecated or obsolete item

Archive / Deprecated References

Examples:

Item

Classification

Destination

Notes

Karma GAP

Tool

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

Supports Milestone-Based Funding and Public Goods Reporting.

EAS

Tool / protocol

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

Supports Impact Attestation and Claim-Evidence Record.

Hypercerts

Tool / protocol

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

Supports Impact Attestation; high evidence-quality risk.

Hylo

Tool / platform

Resource Graph / Tool Registry

Supports Local Discovery Map and Needs / Offers Board.

CSIS

Framework / source reference

Encyclopedia / Concept & Idea Ecology / Deployment

Informs Structural Integrity Review; do not claim conformance by default.

Gitcoin / Allo / Octant / Giveth / Open Collective

Tools / ecosystems / funding infrastructure

Resource Graph

Support funding and allocation options.

SuperBenefit Knowledge Garden / Bloom Wiki / P2P Foundation

Source systems

Source System Registry

Should be credited and revisited as living knowledge environments.

Green Goods

Tool / implementation case

Resource Graph / Implementation Memory

Supports local impact reporting and claim-evidence workflows.

Local regenerative funding round

Deployment / implementation

Deployment / Implementation Memory

May use multiple funding and reporting options.

Knowledge Commoning Swarm participation

Implementation case / source context

Implementation Memory

Can inform Option Library, Resource Graph, and contributor workflows.

Open Questions

  • Which options are most useful for knowledge gardeners right now?
  • Which options are most useful for local regenerative communities?
  • Which options require structural review before being recommended?
  • Which named entries are really tools, not options?
  • Which tools support multiple options?
  • Which options are too heavy for early-stage communities?
  • Which options create privacy, consent, or location risks?
  • Which options risk over-financializing regenerative work?
  • Which options are already well documented by other toolkits?
  • Which options should reference Crypto Altruists, OpenCivics, Bloom, SuperBenefit, P2P Foundation, Metagov, ReFi DAO, GreenPill, CSIS, or other source systems?
  • Which options should become templates?
  • Which options should remain conceptual for now?
  • What option entries need field-informed examples before they are treated as mature?

Working Summary

The Option Library helps communities move from vague intention to visible design space.

It preserves options as reusable design choices, while routing tools, resources, source systems, concepts, deployments, cases, and signals to their proper homes.

A strong Option Library makes it easier to compare approaches, see dependencies, avoid failure modes, preserve source lineage, identify structural risks, and move toward deployment with care.

The next step is not to add more options. The next step is to develop the highest-priority options into full entries, route tool/resource entries into the Resource Graph, and connect field-tested deployments back into Implementation Memory.

🧰 Deployment & Structural Integrity

Purpose

Deployment is where knowledge, resources, concepts, and options become operational.

The Deployment & Structural Integrity section helps communities make structure explicit before moving from ideas into action.

It is especially important when the Toolkit informs:

  • local nodes
  • funding rounds
  • knowledge commons assemblies
  • public resource maps
  • community directories
  • governance / coordination systems
  • ecological impact claims
  • AI-assisted discovery
  • public goods programs
  • local regenerative projects
  • research sprints
  • ecosystem mapping initiatives
  • implementation cases

Deployment asks:

What must be visible, agreed, reviewed, or protected before this becomes active in the world?

Core distinction

An option is not a deployment.

Object

Meaning

Option

A reusable design choice.

Tool

A specific platform, protocol, or component.

Deployment

A context-specific assembly of options, tools, roles, obligations, boundaries, and review pathways.

Implementation

What actually happens when the deployment is used in practice.

Example:

  • Option: milestone-based funding
  • Tool: Karma GAP
  • Deployment: a local regenerative grants process using milestone-based funding, a review committee, a shared treasury, public reporting, and escalation paths
  • Implementation: the actual funding round, including proposals, decisions, updates, conflicts, delays, lessons, and adaptations

Deployment is the bridge between design space and reality.

What deployment should make explicit

A deployment should clarify:

  • purpose
  • scope
  • participants
  • roles
  • decision paths
  • coordination structure
  • source of truth
  • information requirements
  • consent conditions
  • power and control points
  • stewardship obligations
  • review pathways
  • adverse signal channels
  • escalation paths
  • implementation boundaries
  • maintenance responsibilities
  • evidence expectations
  • privacy boundaries
  • source lineage
  • unresolved questions
  • structural risks
  • regenerative obligations

The goal is not bureaucracy.
The goal is legibility.

Deployment entry template

Field

Description

Deployment name

Name of the project, process, local node, round, map, or system.

Purpose

Why this exists.

Context

Local, community, ecosystem, technical, or institutional context.

Scope

What is included and excluded.

Participants

Who is involved.

Cost-bearing parties

Who may be affected or carry risk.

Selected options

Reusable patterns being used.

Tools / components

Specific platforms, protocols, or resources.

Roles

Stewards, reviewers, contributors, signers, facilitators, maintainers, participants.

Decision paths

How decisions are made and recorded.

Source of truth

Where reliable information lives.

Information requirements

What people need to know to participate meaningfully.

Information asymmetries

Who knows what others do not.

Consent requirements

What permissions or agreements are needed.

Power / control points

Who controls funds, infrastructure, data, moderation, or narrative.

Adverse signal channels

Where concerns, harms, failures, or objections go.

Named response

What happens when a concern is raised.

Review cadence

When and how the deployment is reviewed.

Maintenance responsibilities

Who keeps it working.

Evidence / reporting

What should be captured.

Maturity status

Draft, candidate, active, reviewed, field-informed, deprecated.

Open questions

What is unresolved.

Lightweight deployment template

For early use:

Question

Response

What are we trying to do?

Who is involved?

Who could be affected?

What options or tools are we using?

Who decides what?

Where is the source of truth?

What consent is needed?

Who controls funds, data, infrastructure, or publication?

What can go wrong?

Where do concerns go?

Who responds?

What should we learn from this?

Structural Integrity

Core idea

Structural integrity asks whether the system’s basic conditions are visible enough to function without hidden harm.

A deployment may have good intentions and still be structurally weak.

Structural weakness can appear as:

  • vague roles
  • unclear decision paths
  • hidden power
  • missing consent
  • weak review
  • unclear ownership
  • unclear maintenance
  • information asymmetry
  • invisible labor
  • no adverse signal path
  • unclear funding control
  • overclaiming
  • poor source lineage
  • weak evidence
  • no way to update after failure

The Toolkit should help detect these weaknesses early.

CSIS-informed posture

The Toolkit should be informed by CSIS and adjacent structural integrity work.

The current posture is:

CSIS-informed, not CSIS-conformant by default.

That means CSIS can inform vocabulary, checks, audits, and structural design, but the Toolkit should not claim formal conformance unless specific criteria, review process, and results are documented.

The useful distinction:

CSIS helps detect structural exposure.
The Toolkit defines how to respond.

Detection is not enough. If something is found to be structurally weak, the deployment needs a named response.

Structural floors and generative capacities

Deployment should hold two kinds of requirements.

Structural floors

Structural floors are minimum conditions that reduce confusion, exploitation, hidden power, and coordination failure.

Examples:

  • clear purpose
  • clear roles
  • source of truth
  • visible decision paths
  • consent boundaries
  • power/control visibility
  • source lineage
  • review pathways
  • adverse signal channels
  • maintenance responsibilities
  • fixed / configurable / experimental boundaries

Generative capacities

Generative capacities allow the system to learn, adapt, repair, and remain alive.

Examples:

  • sensemaking
  • trust-building
  • conflict transformation
  • contributor care
  • local adaptation
  • pattern harvesting
  • learning from implementation
  • memory
  • regenerative obligation
  • unresolved tension tracking

A deployment with only structural floors may become rigid.
A deployment with only generative capacity may become vague and dependent on informal labor.

Good deployment needs both.

Deployment Checklist

1. Purpose and scope

Clarify what is being deployed.

Questions:

  • What is the purpose?
  • What problem, opportunity, or need does this address?
  • What is included?
  • What is excluded?
  • What assumptions are being made?
  • What would make this successful?
  • What would make this harmful or unnecessary?
  • What should not be overclaimed?

Output:

  • purpose statement
  • scope boundary
  • explicit exclusions
  • success / failure criteria
  • open assumptions

2. Context

Clarify the setting.

Questions:

  • Is this local, digital, bioregional, organizational, public, private, or hybrid?
  • What community or ecosystem is involved?
  • What local context matters?
  • What cultural context matters?
  • What legal or institutional context matters?
  • What ecological context matters?
  • What prior work does this build on?
  • What relationships already exist?
  • What tensions or histories should be known?

Output:

  • context note
  • relevant history
  • local / cultural / ecological considerations
  • related source systems or prior work

3. Participants and cost-bearing parties

Clarify who is involved and who may be affected.

Questions:

  • Who is participating?
  • Who is contributing?
  • Who is deciding?
  • Who is reviewing?
  • Who is maintaining?
  • Who is represented?
  • Who may be affected?
  • Who may bear costs without direct control?
  • Who should be consulted?
  • Who should be able to contest representation?

Output:

  • participant map
  • role list
  • cost-bearing party note
  • consultation requirements

4. Roles and obligations

Clarify who does what and where obligations run.

Questions:

  • What roles are required?
  • What does each role control?
  • What is each role responsible for?
  • Who does each role hold obligation toward?
  • How are roles assigned?
  • How are roles reviewed?
  • How can someone leave a role?
  • What happens if a role becomes inactive?
  • What roles are overloaded?

Output:

  • role map
  • obligation direction notes
  • role handoff process
  • capacity risks

5. Decision paths

Clarify how decisions happen.

Questions:

  • What decisions need to be made?
  • Who can make each decision?
  • What requires consent, vote, review, or delegation?
  • What decisions are reversible?
  • What decisions are high-impact?
  • Where are decisions recorded?
  • How can decisions be challenged?
  • What happens in emergencies?

Output:

  • decision map
  • decision log
  • escalation path
  • emergency process

6. Information requirements and asymmetries

Clarify what information is needed and who has it.

Questions:

  • What information do participants need to participate meaningfully?
  • Who has context others lack?
  • Who understands the technical system?
  • Who understands the local context?
  • Who controls documentation?
  • What information is hidden, implicit, or hard to interpret?
  • What information asymmetries could create unfairness or harm?
  • What must be explained before participation?

Output:

  • minimum context note
  • information asymmetry map
  • onboarding requirements
  • documentation needs

7. Consent and representation

Clarify permissions and visibility.

Questions:

  • Who or what is being represented?
  • Has consent been given?
  • Consent for what specific use?
  • Is consent ongoing or one-time?
  • Can consent be withdrawn?
  • Are people, communities, places, or local knowledge being mapped?
  • What should remain private?
  • What should be anonymized or aggregated?
  • Is AI being used on personal, community, or location data?

Output:

  • consent checklist
  • visibility settings
  • withdrawal process
  • representation notes

8. Power and control

Clarify where control lives.

Questions:

  • Who controls funds?
  • Who controls infrastructure?
  • Who controls admin access?
  • Who controls data?
  • Who controls publishing?
  • Who controls moderation?
  • Who controls final interpretation?
  • Who controls public narrative?
  • Who can pause, revise, or shut down the deployment?
  • What power is informal but real?

Output:

  • power/control map
  • admin/access list
  • treasury control note
  • infrastructure dependency note
  • informal power risks

9. Source lineage and attribution

Clarify where knowledge came from.

Questions:

  • What sources are being used?
  • Who created them?
  • Are we quoting, summarizing, adapting, or synthesizing?
  • What source systems should be credited?
  • What communities should be acknowledged?
  • Is any knowledge being extracted from a local or community context?
  • What return path exists?
  • What correction path exists?

Output:

  • source lineage notes
  • attribution list
  • use type labels
  • correction path
  • return path where appropriate

10. Evidence and claims

Clarify what is being claimed.

Questions:

  • What claims are being made?
  • Are these claims conceptual, social, ecological, financial, operational, or technical?
  • What evidence supports them?
  • What evidence is missing?
  • Who reviewed the evidence?
  • What uncertainty remains?
  • What should not be claimed publicly?
  • Are metrics being overused or misrepresented?

Output:

  • claim-evidence table
  • evidence quality note
  • review status
  • uncertainty note

11. Adverse signals and named response

Clarify what happens when something goes wrong.

Questions:

  • What adverse signals should be watched?
  • Where can concerns be raised?
  • Who receives them?
  • Who is obligated to respond?
  • What response options exist?
  • What happens if the issue is ignored?
  • What gets documented?
  • What remains private?
  • What requires escalation?
  • What requires pause or redesign?

Output:

  • adverse signal channel
  • response steward
  • response options
  • escalation path
  • unresolved issue log

12. Review and update cadence

Clarify how the deployment learns.

Questions:

  • When is the deployment reviewed?
  • Who reviews it?
  • What evidence is reviewed?
  • How are signals interpreted?
  • How are updates made?
  • What becomes implementation memory?
  • What gets routed back into the Toolkit?
  • What should be deprecated, revised, or repeated?

Output:

  • review cadence
  • retrospective template
  • update process
  • implementation memory routing

Structural Exposure Card

A Structural Exposure Card is a short summary of unresolved risk.

It should be used when something proceeds even though a structural gap, uncertainty, or risk remains.

Template

Field

Response

Deployment / object

What is being assessed?

Exposure identified

What is structurally weak, unclear, or unresolved?

Affected parties

Who may be affected or bear costs?

Severity

Low, medium, high, unknown.

Why proceeding may still make sense

Context or rationale.

Required disclosure

What should be visible to participants or users?

Named steward

Who is responsible for monitoring or response?

Review date

When this must be revisited.

Possible response

Revise, pause, narrow scope, add consent, add review, archive, continue with disclosure.

Example exposures

  • resource map includes communities without confirmed consent
  • AI-assisted recommendations are active before bias review
  • funding round has unclear appeal path
  • ecological impact claims lack sufficient evidence
  • public directory has no correction path
  • deployment depends on one overloaded maintainer
  • source system is heavily used without clear attribution
  • local project is represented by outsiders
  • governance process names participation but decision power remains elsewhere

High-Risk Deployment Triggers

Some deployments require stronger review.

High-risk conditions

Use stronger review when the deployment involves:

  • funds or resource allocation
  • public claims about impact
  • ecological, carbon, biodiversity, or restoration claims
  • personal profiles
  • community profiles
  • location data
  • vulnerable communities
  • AI-assisted matching or recommendations
  • governance / coordination decisions
  • public representation of local groups
  • legal, tax, or institutional claims
  • sensitive cultural or local knowledge
  • identity, reputation, or contribution history
  • public rankings or scoring
  • conflict mediation
  • external funder reporting

Required review types

Depending on context, require:

  • source review
  • community review
  • privacy review
  • legal / tax review
  • ecological review
  • structural integrity review
  • AI review
  • implementation review
  • place-based review
  • conflict / facilitation review

Regenerative Obligation in Deployment

Deployment should ask not only “Does this work?” but also “Toward whom do we hold obligation?”

Possible obligation directions

A deployment may hold obligation toward:

  • contributors
  • reviewers
  • local communities
  • source communities
  • people represented in maps
  • funders
  • participants
  • maintainers
  • future users
  • affected ecosystems
  • knowledge holders
  • public institutions
  • cost-bearing parties
  • places and bioregions

Regenerative obligation questions

  • Who made this possible?
  • Who should be credited?
  • Who should be asked before publication?
  • Who may bear costs?
  • Who could be misrepresented?
  • Who should have a correction path?
  • What local context must be preserved?
  • What should not be extracted?
  • What return path is possible?
  • What would make this reciprocal rather than extractive?

Place Profile Template

Use this when a deployment is local, regional, ecological, or bioregional.

Field

Description

Place name

City, region, watershed, bioregion, territory, or community context.

Ecological context

Ecosystem, watershed, species, climate, restoration context, land use.

Cultural / historical context

Local history, community context, relevant tensions.

Current stewards

People, communities, organizations, or institutions active in the place.

Existing projects

Local initiatives, maps, field work, events, campaigns.

Local knowledge holders

People or groups whose knowledge matters.

Sensitive knowledge

What should not be public or should require consent.

Local assets

Spaces, tools, skills, institutions, community resources.

Local needs

Requests, gaps, vulnerabilities, opportunities.

Consent requirements

What representation requires local agreement.

Risks

Ecological, political, social, financial, or privacy risks.

Related resources

Maps, datasets, reports, community docs, source systems.

Review needs

Ecological, community, legal, cultural, or structural review.

Deployment Maturity Ladder

Level

State

Meaning

0

Informal

Intent exists, but key structures are implicit.

1

Minimally legible

Purpose, participants, source of truth, and basic roles are visible.

2

Operationally structured

Decision paths, consent, review, power/control, and maintenance are defined.

3

Structurally reviewed

Reviewed against structural integrity criteria or equivalent.

4

Field-tested

Used in practice and generated implementation signals.

5

Pattern-generating

Produced reusable lessons, templates, failure notes, or patterns for the commons.

This maturity ladder is not a ranking of virtue.
It helps people know how much trust to place in a deployment.

Example Deployment Types

Knowledge commons assembly

Possible components:

  • public docs
  • resource graph
  • contribution guide
  • review process
  • source lineage metadata
  • glossary
  • open-question pages
  • synthesis rhythm
  • update process

Key risks:

  • poor review
  • stale content
  • unclear attribution
  • over-reliance on one maintainer
  • AI-generated false authority
  • weak contributor pathways

Resource map

Possible components:

  • resource registry
  • categories
  • relationship grammar
  • source systems
  • review states
  • public interface
  • contribution form

Key risks:

  • link dump
  • outdated entries
  • poor source lineage
  • community representation without consent
  • sensitive location exposure
  • no correction path

Local node

Possible components:

  • local profile
  • roles
  • event rhythm
  • resource map
  • request / offer board
  • decision path
  • funding pathway
  • implementation memory
  • local consent rules

Key risks:

  • local node drift
  • dependency on one organizer
  • weak local legitimacy
  • imported models
  • poor documentation
  • hidden power
  • burnout

Funding round

Possible components:

  • eligibility rules
  • application form
  • review process
  • allocation mechanism
  • treasury process
  • reporting system
  • appeal path
  • impact evidence
  • public updates

Key risks:

  • gaming
  • unclear authority
  • weak review
  • delayed payments
  • metric theater
  • conflict of interest
  • grant dependency
  • no adverse signal path

Public community directory

Possible components:

  • community profiles
  • contact preferences
  • visibility settings
  • consent fields
  • correction path
  • moderation process
  • update cadence

Key risks:

  • representation without consent
  • outdated profiles
  • unwanted contact
  • location exposure
  • AI-assisted recommendations without permission
  • no correction process

Ecological impact project

Possible components:

  • place profile
  • ecological context
  • local stewards
  • evidence plan
  • monitoring process
  • claims policy
  • consent process
  • reporting rhythm
  • funding path

Key risks:

  • ecological overclaiming
  • poor evidence
  • carbon tunnel vision
  • local knowledge extraction
  • restoration without maintenance
  • external funder distortion
  • remote sensing mismatch

Deployment Outputs

A well-formed deployment should produce at least some of the following:

  • deployment profile
  • role map
  • decision map
  • consent checklist
  • source lineage notes
  • power/control map
  • adverse signal path
  • review cadence
  • maintenance plan
  • evidence plan
  • structural exposure card
  • implementation memory plan
  • open questions list

Not every deployment needs every output. The depth should match the risk.

Working Summary

Deployment is the Toolkit’s discipline of moving from possibility into practice.

It helps prevent communities from treating attractive tools, concepts, or mechanisms as ready-made solutions.

A good deployment process makes visible:

  • what is being attempted
  • who is involved
  • who may be affected
  • who controls what
  • what consent is required
  • what information is asymmetric
  • what claims are being made
  • what can go wrong
  • where signals go
  • who responds
  • how learning returns to the commons

The goal is not to slow everything down.

The goal is to move with enough structure that the work can remain trustworthy, adaptive, and regenerative.

🧬 Implementation Memory & Evolution

Purpose

Implementation Memory and Evolution help the Toolkit learn from practice.

A knowledge commons becomes more useful when it does not only collect concepts, resources, and options, but also captures what happens when people actually try to use them.

This section preserves:

  • implementation cases
  • field notes
  • retrospectives
  • failures
  • adaptations
  • signals
  • unresolved tensions
  • pattern candidates
  • updates to the Toolkit

Implementation Memory asks:

What happened in practice?

Evolution asks:

What should the commons learn from it?

1. Implementation Memory

Function

Implementation Memory captures concrete attempts to coordinate, build, fund, map, govern, restore, learn, or organize.

It is the layer where ideas meet real conditions.

This includes:

  • local nodes
  • knowledge gardens
  • resource maps
  • ecosystem atlases
  • funding rounds
  • public goods programs
  • research sprints
  • hackathons
  • community campaigns
  • governance / coordination processes
  • civic partnerships
  • ecological restoration projects
  • workshops and events
  • AI-assisted curation workflows
  • tool experiments
  • contributor systems
  • local finance experiments
  • implementation failures

Implementation Memory should preserve not only what worked, but also what was confusing, fragile, contested, or incomplete.

Why this matters

Without implementation memory, the Toolkit risks becoming theoretical.

People may read about concepts, frameworks, and options without knowing:

  • where they have been tried
  • what conditions made them work
  • what failed
  • what required hidden labor
  • what created conflict
  • what was overclaimed
  • what was misunderstood
  • what became useful only after adaptation
  • what should not be repeated

Implementation Memory helps prevent the same mistakes from being repeated across communities.

What counts as an implementation

An implementation is any concrete use of ideas, resources, options, or tools in a real context.

Examples:

Implementation type

Description

Local node

A local group coordinating regenerative activity.

Knowledge commons assembly

A community building or maintaining shared knowledge.

Resource map

A structured map of tools, people, projects, or communities.

Funding round

A process allocating resources to projects or contributors.

Research sprint

A time-bound effort to investigate, synthesize, or produce knowledge.

Workshop / event

A gathering that produces learning, relationships, outputs, or signals.

Governance / coordination process

A system for proposals, roles, decisions, or stewardship.

Ecological project

A restoration, monitoring, agroforestry, climate, or land-based initiative.

Public goods program

A program supporting open-source, civic, or regenerative work.

AI workflow

A process using AI for synthesis, classification, review, or discovery.

Tool experiment

Testing a platform, protocol, schema, or interface.

Failure case

A breakdown that produced important lessons.

Implementation entry template

Field

Description

Name

Name of implementation, project, event, round, or case.

Type

Local node, funding round, resource map, research sprint, etc.

Context

Where and why it happened.

Participants

People, groups, communities, organizations, or roles involved.

Purpose

What it was trying to do.

Selected options

Patterns or design choices used.

Tools / components

Specific tools, platforms, protocols, docs, or infrastructure.

Source lineage

What prior work, communities, or resources informed it.

Timeline

When it happened or current status.

Outputs

What was produced.

Outcomes

What changed as a result.

Signals

What was learned, noticed, questioned, or contested.

Failures / frictions

What did not work or created difficulty.

Adaptations

What changed during or after implementation.

Evidence

Reports, links, notes, data, attestations, screenshots, recordings, etc.

Review status

Raw, draft, reviewed, field-informed, pattern-generating, etc.

Open questions

What remains unresolved.

Toolkit updates

What this case should change in the Toolkit.

Lightweight implementation note

For early use:

Question

Response

What happened?

Who was involved?

What was the purpose?

What tools or options were used?

What worked?

What did not work?

What surprised us?

What should be remembered?

What should update the Toolkit?

2. Implementation Evidence

Function

Implementation evidence helps the Toolkit distinguish between theory, preference, narrative, and field learning.

Evidence does not need to be only quantitative. In complex social, ecological, and community contexts, useful evidence can include stories, reflections, field observations, tensions, records, and qualitative signals.

Evidence types

Evidence type

Examples

Output evidence

Docs, maps, reports, tools, events, proposals, code, templates.

Participation evidence

Attendance, contributors, roles, submissions, interactions.

Financial evidence

Grants, budgets, disbursements, matching funds, treasury records.

Process evidence

Decision logs, meeting notes, review records, role maps.

Impact evidence

Outcomes, metrics, public goods produced, ecological changes, social benefits.

Field evidence

Photos, observations, site notes, biodiversity records, restoration logs.

Community evidence

Participant reflections, feedback, local validation, testimonials.

Adverse signals

Complaints, conflicts, harms, confusion, objections, drop-off, burnout.

Adaptation evidence

Changes made in response to signals.

Learning evidence

Retrospectives, postmortems, pattern harvests, unresolved questions.

Evidence quality

Not all evidence carries the same trust level.

Evidence should be described with enough context that others can interpret it.

Useful questions:

  • What does this evidence actually show?
  • Who collected it?
  • Who interpreted it?
  • What context is missing?
  • Is the evidence qualitative, quantitative, or mixed?
  • Is it self-reported?
  • Was it reviewed?
  • Is it linked to a claim?
  • What uncertainty remains?
  • What should not be inferred from it?

Claim-evidence discipline

The Toolkit should separate claims from evidence.

Claim

Evidence

Confidence

Notes

What is being asserted?

What supports it?

Low / medium / high / unknown

What uncertainty remains?

Examples:

  • “This workshop onboarded new contributors” should link to attendance, submissions, reflections, or follow-up participation.
  • “This project improved biodiversity” should link to ecological observations, monitoring data, expert review, or field evidence.
  • “This funding process improved accountability” should link to reports, milestones, payments, disputes, or participant feedback.
  • “This governance model worked” should clarify for whom, under what conditions, and with what tensions.

Working goal

Evidence should support learning without creating false certainty.

3. Signals

Function

Signals are observations from use.

They may be positive, negative, ambiguous, weak, repeated, or unresolved.

A signal is not automatically a conclusion. It is something that asks to be interpreted.

Signal types

Signal type

Description

Learning signal

Something that clarifies how a system works.

Adverse signal

Something indicating harm, risk, conflict, exclusion, or failure.

Confusion signal

Something people repeatedly misunderstand.

Capacity signal

Something indicating burnout, overload, or maintenance limits.

Participation signal

Something showing engagement, drop-off, or exclusion.

Source signal

Something indicating missing attribution, broken links, or weak lineage.

Structural signal

Something revealing unclear roles, decisions, consent, or power.

Implementation signal

Something learned by doing.

Ecological signal

Something observed in a living system or place.

Funding signal

Something related to incentives, reporting, payments, or allocation.

AI signal

Something related to hallucination, flattening, bias, or source loss.

Drift signal

Something indicating movement away from regenerative purpose.

Pattern signal

Something repeated enough to become a candidate pattern.

Signal entry template

Field

Description

Signal

What was observed?

Source

Where did it come from?

Implementation / context

Where did it appear?

Type

Learning, adverse, structural, capacity, ecological, etc.

Affected layer

Encyclopedia, Resource Graph, Option Library, Deployment, etc.

Severity

Low, medium, high, unknown.

Who is affected

Participants, contributors, local community, funders, users, ecosystem, etc.

Interpretation

What might it mean?

Response needed

None, review, update, escalation, pause, redesign, archive.

Status

Raw, under review, interpreted, acted on, unresolved.

Follow-up

What should happen next?

Adverse signals

Adverse signals deserve special care.

Examples:

  • people feel misrepresented
  • contributors are burning out
  • a funding process is being gamed
  • local knowledge is being extracted
  • ecological claims are weak
  • AI output is misleading
  • decision power is hidden
  • a public map exposes sensitive information
  • a community lacks correction path
  • documentation creates false authority
  • a tool creates dependency or lock-in
  • a process excludes people unintentionally
  • conflict is being hidden rather than addressed

Adverse signals should not disappear into private memory.

They should have:

  • a channel
  • a steward
  • a response path
  • a status
  • a decision about what can be public or private
  • a way to update the commons where appropriate

4. Retrospectives

Function

Retrospectives turn experience into learning.

They should happen after:

  • events
  • funding rounds
  • research sprints
  • local projects
  • mapping efforts
  • governance / coordination processes
  • tool experiments
  • ecological interventions
  • major publication cycles
  • AI-assisted synthesis rounds

Retrospectives should not only ask whether the work succeeded. They should ask what the work revealed.

Retrospective template

Question

Response

What did we try to do?

What actually happened?

What worked?

What did not work?

What created confusion?

What created energy?

What created friction?

What hidden labor appeared?

What assumptions changed?

What signals emerged?

What should be repeated?

What should not be repeated?

What should be updated in the Toolkit?

What remains unresolved?

Who should be credited?

Who should be consulted before sharing?

Retrospective outputs

A good retrospective may produce:

  • implementation case update
  • failure mode
  • pattern candidate
  • open question
  • revised option
  • revised deployment template
  • resource entry
  • concept clarification
  • source lineage correction
  • maturity status update
  • structural exposure card
  • contributor care note
  • infrastructure note

5. Pattern Harvesting

Function

Pattern harvesting turns repeated or transferable learning into reusable guidance.

A pattern is not just something that happened once.
A pattern is something that may help others act more wisely in related contexts.

When evidence becomes a pattern

Evidence may become a pattern when:

  • similar signals repeat across multiple cases
  • an implementation is documented clearly enough for others to understand transfer conditions
  • a failure mode appears repeatedly
  • a specific option works under identifiable conditions
  • a local adaptation reveals a generalizable design principle
  • multiple communities independently converge on a similar practice
  • a structural risk recurs across deployments

Pattern entry template

Field

Description

Pattern name

Short name.

Problem context

Where this pattern appears.

Pattern summary

What tends to work or recur.

Conditions

When it applies.

Does not apply when

Contexts where it may fail.

Examples

Cases or implementations.

Evidence quality

Weak, medium, strong, mixed, anecdotal.

Related options

Option Library entries.

Related failure modes

What can go wrong.

Structural notes

Consent, power, review, roles, maintenance.

Open questions

What remains uncertain.

Anti-patterns

An anti-pattern is a recurring design that looks useful but tends to create harm, fragility, or hidden cost.

Examples:

  • resource dump without stewardship
  • community map without consent
  • AI summary without source lineage
  • governance process without real decision power
  • token incentives without local trust
  • funding round without appeal path
  • impact claim without evidence
  • local node dependent on one organizer
  • public directory without correction path
  • review system without reviewer care
  • ecosystem map that credits aggregators but not sources

Anti-patterns should be treated as valuable learning.

6. Evolution Layer

Function

The Evolution Layer turns signals and implementation learning into updates.

It helps decide what should change in:

  • Ontology
  • Encyclopedia
  • Resource Graph
  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Option Library
  • Deployment templates
  • Review & Maturity
  • Infrastructure
  • Discovery & Affinity
  • Regenerative Obligation
  • Open Questions
  • Implementation Memory itself

Evolution is not automatic. It requires interpretation.

Evolution process

A simple evolution process:

  1. Receive signal
  2. Classify signal
  3. Identify affected layer
  4. Interpret meaning
  5. Decide response
  6. Update, archive, escalate, or preserve as unresolved
  7. Record source and rationale
  8. Communicate changes where needed

Evolution routing table

Signal

Possible route

Missing concept

Encyclopedia or Concept & Idea Ecology

Confusing term

Glossary, Ontology, bridge-language note

Repeated resource need

Resource Graph entry or source system card

New reusable practice

Option Library candidate

Structural risk

Deployment checklist or Structural Exposure Card

Failure mode

Anti-pattern Library

Implementation lesson

Implementation Memory and pattern harvest

Weak evidence

Review & Maturity update

Privacy concern

Privacy, Consent & Identity update

Source attribution issue

Provenance & Attribution update

Tool limitation

Infrastructure note

Local context concern

Place Profile or Regenerative Obligation update

AI error

AI review process or source-lineage note

Funding issue

Funding option risk note

Ecological uncertainty

Evidence / MRV review note

Repeated tension

Open question or unresolved tension log

Response types

Possible responses:

  • update page
  • add resource
  • revise definition
  • add source
  • add warning
  • add open question
  • revise option
  • add structural check
  • change maturity state
  • add failure mode
  • archive outdated content
  • request review
  • escalate to steward
  • create pattern candidate
  • create implementation case
  • pause public use
  • preserve as unresolved

Update record template

Field

Description

Change made

What changed?

Reason

Why?

Signal source

What triggered the change?

Affected sections

What was updated?

Contributor / steward

Who made or reviewed the update?

Date

When?

Maturity impact

Did review status change?

Open follow-up

What still needs attention?

7. Unresolved Tension Log

Function

Some tensions should remain visible rather than being forced into premature decisions.

The Toolkit should preserve uncertainty when:

  • evidence is incomplete
  • communities disagree
  • terms are contested
  • practices are context-dependent
  • risks are not fully understood
  • implementation experience is mixed
  • ethical tradeoffs remain open
  • infrastructure choices are premature

Tension log template

Field

Description

Tension

What is unresolved?

Why it matters

Why this should stay visible.

Related sections

Where it connects.

Current interpretations

Main perspectives.

Risks of resolving too early

What could go wrong if forced.

What evidence is needed

What would help clarify it.

Status

Open, under review, partially resolved, archived.

Example tensions

  • open knowledge vs protected knowledge
  • discovery vs surveillance
  • local specificity vs reusable pattern
  • AI synthesis vs source fidelity
  • funding mechanisms vs regenerative values
  • ecological metrics vs living systems complexity
  • contributor openness vs review quality
  • decentralization vs stewardship responsibility
  • global public goods funding vs local accountability
  • knowledge graph precision vs contributor burden
  • community visibility vs community safety

8. Implementation-to-Commons Loop

The Toolkit should make the path from practice back to knowledge explicit.

Loop

  1. A community tries something.
  2. The implementation is documented.
  3. Signals are captured.
  4. Retrospective or sensemaking happens.
  5. Patterns, failures, and questions are identified.
  6. Relevant Toolkit sections are updated.
  7. Source lineage and contributors are credited.
  8. Future communities can learn from the case.

Example

A local node runs a small regenerative funding round.

Implementation Memory captures:

  • purpose
  • roles
  • funding mechanism
  • tools used
  • proposal process
  • review process
  • reporting
  • conflicts
  • delays
  • lessons

Signals reveal:

  • applicants did not understand eligibility
  • reviewers lacked enough context
  • one signer controlled too much timing
  • milestone reporting helped transparency
  • ecological claims needed better evidence
  • local participants preferred simpler language

Evolution routes updates to:

  • Funding options
  • Deployment checklist
  • Glossary
  • Review & Maturity
  • Evidence / MRV guidance
  • Local node patterns
  • Failure modes
  • Future onboarding guide

The commons becomes smarter because practice was remembered.

9. Implementation Memory Categories

Local coordination cases

Examples:

  • local GreenPill chapters
  • Bloom local communities
  • bioregional projects
  • local regenerative hubs
  • community workshops
  • mutual aid systems
  • local currency experiments

Funding and allocation cases

Examples:

  • Gitcoin rounds
  • Octant rounds
  • local matching pools
  • Gardens conviction voting pools
  • milestone-based grants
  • direct grants
  • retroactive funding
  • community currencies
  • public goods reporting systems

Knowledge commons cases

Examples:

  • SuperBenefit Knowledge Garden
  • Bloom Wiki
  • OpenCivics commons materials
  • Regen Toolkit
  • decentralized curation projects
  • community resource maps
  • shared glossaries
  • component indexes

Ecological and MRV cases

Examples:

  • restoration projects
  • agroforestry projects
  • biodiversity mapping
  • community science efforts
  • MRV experiments
  • tree planting protocols
  • farmOS-style record keeping
  • Restor mapping
  • local ecological monitoring

AI and sensemaking cases

Examples:

  • AI-assisted synthesis workflows
  • Harmonica-style deliberation
  • Bonfires-style group sensemaking
  • semantic search experiments
  • AI-assisted resource classification
  • RAG knowledge assistants
  • event synthesis workflows

Governance / coordination cases

Examples:

  • DAO proposal processes
  • councils
  • working groups
  • Decidim processes
  • Pol.is deliberations
  • vTaiwan-style civic deliberation
  • role-based stewardship
  • conflict transformation pathways

Failure cases

Examples:

  • governance capture
  • token incentive failures
  • documentation decay
  • funding gaming
  • contributor burnout
  • local node drift
  • ecological overclaiming
  • AI hallucination
  • source attribution loss
  • public map harm
  • platform lock-in

10. Working Priorities

1. Create a first implementation case template

The template should be easy enough for people to use after a project, event, or round.

2. Identify existing cases worth capturing

Start with cases already close to the ecosystem:

  • local GreenPill work
  • Regen Toolkit development
  • Gitcoin / Allo / Karma GAP / Hypercerts examples
  • Bloom community practices
  • SuperBenefit knowledge garden practices
  • OpenCivics / Knowledge Commoning Swarm outputs
  • Green Goods / local impact reporting experiments
  • ReFi localism finance examples
  • community science or agroforestry cases

3. Build the anti-pattern library

This may be one of the most valuable early outputs.

Priority anti-patterns:

  • extractive aggregation
  • governance theater
  • AI-assisted false authority
  • local node drift
  • hidden power
  • funding metric theater
  • public map without consent
  • ecological claim without evidence
  • overbuilt knowledge graph
  • contributor burnout
  • tokenized everything
  • documentation without maintenance

4. Add implementation memory to every deployment

Any serious deployment should include a plan for:

  • signal capture
  • retrospective
  • evidence collection
  • source lineage
  • update routing
  • unresolved tension logging

5. Develop pattern-harvest criteria

Clarify when a case becomes a pattern and when it remains only a case.

6. Make adverse signals receivable

Adverse signals need channels and named response, especially for high-risk work involving funds, local communities, public claims, AI recommendations, ecological work, or community representation.

Working Summary

Implementation Memory and Evolution keep the Toolkit alive.

Implementation Memory asks:

What happened, what changed, what failed, what surprised us, and what should be remembered?

Evolution asks:

What does this mean for the commons, and what should be updated?

Together, they prevent the Toolkit from becoming a static map or abstract architecture.

They help the commons become a living system that learns from practice.

🧩 Infrastructure & Interoperability

Purpose

The Infrastructure & Interoperability section explores the technical and social substrates that could support the Regen Knowledge Commons Toolkit.

The Toolkit should not assume that every need requires a new platform.

Some needs may be met through:

  • better documentation;
  • shared schemas;
  • structured tables;
  • Markdown files;
  • public websites;
  • lightweight databases;
  • knowledge graphs;
  • AI-assisted workflows;
  • collaborative documents;
  • community review processes;
  • open standards;
  • existing civic, commons, and web3 tools;
  • decentralized protocols;
  • trust, ranking, and reputation systems;
  • retrieval and sensemaking layers.

Infrastructure should serve the knowledge commons.

It should not define the commons too early.

The goal is to keep the Toolkit substrate-portable: able to move between documents, spreadsheets, Markdown, static sites, graph databases, collaborative tools, AI workflows, decentralized protocols, and trust/reputation systems without losing source lineage, review status, consent boundaries, or community meaning.

This section should preserve optionality.

No single substrate should be treated as the whole commons.

Core Posture

The Toolkit should begin with simple infrastructure and preserve a path toward more structured systems.

A good infrastructure path should be:

  • easy enough for contributors to use;
  • structured enough to avoid becoming a link dump;
  • portable enough to migrate later;
  • source-aware enough to preserve attribution;
  • reviewable enough to avoid false authority;
  • privacy-conscious enough to protect people and communities;
  • interoperable enough to connect with other knowledge systems;
  • flexible enough to support both human-readable and machine-readable forms;
  • modular enough to allow different infrastructure components to be tested independently;
  • cautious enough to avoid premature lock-in;
  • honest enough to treat rankings, AI outputs, and graph relationships as signals, not conclusions.

A Google Doc, spreadsheet, Markdown repo, Astro site, Geo space, KOI graph, Fileverse document, PageIndex retrieval layer, TrustGraph, OpenRank computation layer, or AI-assisted sensemaking platform may each be useful for different functions.

None should be treated as the complete architecture.

1. Infrastructure Layers

The Toolkit may eventually use several infrastructure layers.

These layers do not need to be implemented all at once.

Layer

Function

Possible substrates

Working document layer

Drafting, collaboration, shared editing

Google Docs, Fileverse dDocs, Notion, HackMD, Etherpad

Structured registry layer

Resource Graph, source systems, metadata, routing

Google Sheets, Airtable, Notion database, CSV, GitHub tables

Source-controlled knowledge layer

Versioned pages, templates, schemas, changelogs

GitHub, Git, Markdown, frontmatter

Published knowledge layer

Public website, docs, navigation, search

Astro, Starlight, Quartz, Docusaurus, MkDocs

Graph / relationship layer

Entities, concepts, relationships, source systems

Geo / GRC-20, KOI, Neo4j, RDF, JSON-LD, Wikibase

Governed knowledge layer

Community-curated knowledge spaces and graph edits

Geo, GRC-20, governance processes, curator programs

Sensemaking layer

Synthesis, deliberation, pattern detection, group intelligence

Harmonica, Bonfires, Pol.is, Kialo, SenseMaker, AI-assisted workflows

Retrieval / knowledge access layer

Long-document search, GraphRAG, structured retrieval, knowledge assistants

PageIndex, TrustGraph.ai, LlamaIndex, Haystack, RAGFlow, semantic search

Mapping layer

Ecosystem maps, place maps, relationship maps

Kumu, Graph Commons, QGIS, OpenStreetMap, MapLibre, FigJam

Provenance layer

Source lineage, authorship, use type, review history

W3C PROV, Dublin Core, Git history, frontmatter, sources_used

Identity / consent layer

Access, contributor identity, consent, permissions

Sign-In with Ethereum, Fileverse permissions, Ceramic, DIDs, Verifiable Credentials

Attestation / claim layer

Verifiable claims, contribution records, review attestations, impact claims

EAS, Hypercerts, Verifiable Credentials, TrustGraph.network

Ranking / reputation layer

Contextual trust, ranking, reviewer signals, discovery weighting

OpenRank, TrustGraph.network, Open Source Observer, trust graphs

Archival layer

Long-term preservation, exports, backups

GitHub exports, Internet Archive, IPFS, Filecoin, ZIP / CSV / JSON exports

Interoperability layer

Data exchange, schema mapping, export formats

CSV, JSON, JSON-LD, RDF, DCAT, Schema.org, Open Referral

The infrastructure should remain modular.

The Toolkit may use one substrate for drafting, another for the Resource Graph, another for public publishing, another for long-document retrieval, and another for experimental trust or reputation signals.

2. Infrastructure Families

2.1 Working Document and Collaboration Tools

These tools support drafting, editing, commenting, and early-stage collaboration.

Tool / substrate

Function

Relevance

Notes

Google Docs

Collaborative drafting

Current working substrate

Easy collaboration, but weak structured data and export limitations

Fileverse dDocs

Privacy-conscious collaborative documents

Candidate Google Docs alternative

Useful where privacy, self-sovereignty, and encrypted collaboration matter

Notion

Docs and lightweight databases

Flexible workspace

Useful for early structuring, but watch lock-in and export quality

HackMD

Collaborative Markdown editing

Markdown-native collaboration

Useful for bridging docs and GitHub workflows

Obsidian

Personal / small-team knowledge gardening

Local graph notes

Useful for individual knowledge gardeners, less ideal as shared public infrastructure

Logseq

Outliner and graph notes

Personal / team knowledge graph

Useful for concept mapping and linked notes

Etherpad

Lightweight collaborative editing

Simple open-source writing

Useful for low-friction collective notes

Notes

Google Docs may remain useful for broad accessibility, but it should not become the only source of truth.

Fileverse may be useful for sensitive drafts, contributor coordination, or documents where access control and self-sovereign collaboration matter.

Working documents should eventually route into more structured substrates when they become stable enough.

2.2 Publishing and Documentation Infrastructure

These tools support public-facing knowledge access.

Tool / substrate

Function

Relevance

Notes

Astro

Static site framework

Current / candidate publishing layer

Good for fast public sites and flexible content architecture

Starlight

Astro documentation framework

Current / candidate docs layer

Useful for structured docs, sidebar navigation, and search

Quartz

Digital garden publishing

Candidate knowledge garden layer

Strong fit for linked Markdown gardens

Docusaurus

Documentation framework

Candidate docs layer

Useful for versioned documentation, especially developer-facing docs

MkDocs Material

Documentation framework

Candidate docs layer

Mature and lightweight docs publishing option

GitHub Pages

Static publishing

Simple public deployment

Useful for minimal static publishing

Vercel

Deployment platform

Candidate deployment layer

Useful for fast deployment, but should not be treated as archival storage

Notes

The current Astro / Starlight direction is appropriate for a public knowledge interface.

The Toolkit should preserve a path from:

working docs → Markdown/frontmatter → published site → structured registry → graph / retrieval layers

Publishing should not be confused with stewardship.

A published site can become stale unless review, source lineage, and maintenance rhythms are built into the process.

2.3 Structured Registry and Tabular Infrastructure

These tools support Resource Graph entries, source systems, metadata, routing, and review status.

Tool / substrate

Function

Relevance

Notes

Google Sheets

Simple structured registry

Strong v0.1 candidate

Good for Resource Graph v0.1 and contributor-friendly editing

Airtable

Structured database

Candidate registry layer

Better views and filtering; watch platform lock-in

Notion database

Lightweight structured workspace

Candidate registry layer

Easy for contributors; weaker portability

CSV

Portable tabular export

Interoperability baseline

Simple, durable, easy to transform

JSON

Structured export

Machine-readable registry

Useful for site generation or APIs

OpenRefine

Data cleanup and reconciliation

Registry maintenance

Useful for deduplication, cleanup, and external ID matching

Frictionless Data

Data package standard

Portable tabular data

Useful for packaging Resource Graph exports

Notes

The Minimum Viable Resource Graph should probably begin as a spreadsheet or CSV-backed table.

The goal should be to preserve enough structure that the data can later migrate into Markdown, JSON, JSON-LD, Geo, KOI, Wikibase, RDF, or another graph substrate.

2.4 Knowledge Graph and Relationship Infrastructure

These tools support entities, relationships, schemas, and graph-based navigation.
