  • semantic search
  • KOI-net
  • Metagov KMS tools

Civic and deliberation tools

  • Decidim
  • Pol.is
  • Loomio
  • Consul
  • Your Priorities
  • vTaiwan process
  • Kialo

Web3 tools

  • Allo
  • Karma GAP
  • Hypercerts
  • EAS
  • DAOstar OpenGrants
  • Sign-In with Ethereum
  • Gitcoin Passport
  • Hats Protocol
  • Safe
  • Unlock
  • Superfluid
  • Tableland
  • Ceramic

Local coordination and community tools

  • Hylo
  • Bonfire Networks
  • Discord
  • Telegram
  • Signal
  • Charmverse
  • forums
  • community mapping tools
  • requests/offers tools

Ecology and MRV tools

  • farmOS
  • OpenTEAM
  • Restor
  • GBIF
  • iNaturalist
  • Global Forest Watch
  • QGIS
  • OpenStreetMap
  • OpenMRV

AI and sensemaking tools

  • Harmonica
  • Bonfires
  • semantic search tools
  • RAG workflows
  • AI-assisted classification
  • OpenMined-style privacy-preserving AI
  • SenseMaker

7. Open Tensions

These tensions should remain visible.

Open knowledge vs protected knowledge

The Toolkit should promote open knowledge while respecting privacy, consent, local knowledge, and sensitive community information.

Ecosystem mapping vs exposure

Mapping helps people discover each other, but can expose people, places, relationships, vulnerabilities, and informal networks.

AI assistance vs source fidelity

AI can accelerate synthesis, but can also erase source lineage, flatten disagreement, or produce polished falsehoods.

Web3 public goods vs regenerative practice

Web3 funding and coordination tools can help regenerative work, but can also over-financialize it.

Local specificity vs reusable pattern

The Toolkit wants reusable options, but regenerative work is context-sensitive.

Structure vs emergence

Too little structure hides power and overburdens informal labor. Too much structure can block participation and adaptation.

Review quality vs contributor ease

Strong review improves trust, but heavy review can block contribution.

Metrics vs meaning

Evidence is necessary, but metrics can distort social and ecological realities.

Public visibility vs community safety

Public directories, maps, and case studies can be useful, but not everything should be public.

Infrastructure sophistication vs maintainability

Advanced graph, AI, or web3 systems may be powerful, but can create maintenance burdens and exclusion.

8. Candidate Research Outputs

This backlog can generate many kinds of outputs.

Short-form outputs

  • glossary entry
  • resource entry
  • source system card
  • open-question note
  • comparison table
  • tool profile
  • concept stub
  • risk note
  • failure mode
  • relationship map

Medium-form outputs

  • Encyclopedia page
  • option entry
  • deployment checklist
  • implementation case
  • ecosystem map section
  • tool comparison
  • concept lineage map
  • evidence review note

Long-form outputs

  • research memo
  • field guide
  • source system review
  • infrastructure evaluation
  • implementation retrospective
  • pattern library
  • ecosystem atlas
  • deployment playbook
  • regenerative knowledge stewardship guide

9. Prioritization Method

Use a simple prioritization method.

High priority

Questions that affect:

  • current document structure
  • Resource Graph design
  • source lineage
  • deployment risk
  • consent and privacy
  • ecological or legal claims
  • Swarm participation
  • immediate contributor workflows
  • high-value missing ecosystem maps

Medium priority

Questions that affect:

  • future infrastructure
  • advanced graph design
  • AI workflows
  • deep concept lineage
  • tool comparisons
  • broader ecosystem expansion

Low priority

Questions that are interesting but not blocking current collaboration.

Priority questions

Ask:

  • Does this block the next iteration?
  • Does this affect safety or trust?
  • Does this improve contributor experience?
  • Does this prevent duplicated work?
  • Does this help people act?
  • Does this improve source lineage?
  • Does this reveal important ecosystem context?
  • Does this help the Toolkit learn from implementation?

10. Research Workflow

Step 1 — Capture

Add the question without trying to solve it immediately.

Step 2 — Route

Identify the relevant Toolkit layer.

Step 3 — Source

List possible source systems, people, communities, or resources.

Step 4 — Investigate

Use appropriate method:

  • desk research
  • source review
  • interviews
  • tool test
  • case review
  • workshop
  • ecosystem scan
  • implementation experiment

Step 5 — Synthesize

Produce an output that can update the Toolkit.

Step 6 — Update

Route the synthesis into:

  • Encyclopedia
  • Resource Graph
  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Option Library
  • Deployment
  • Implementation Memory
  • Infrastructure
  • Review & Maturity

Step 7 — Preserve uncertainty

If unresolved, keep the question visible.

Working Summary

The Open Questions & Research Backlog protects the Toolkit from false closure.

It gives the commons a place to hold uncertainty, curiosity, tensions, missing research, and future work.

A strong backlog does not mean the Toolkit is incomplete in a bad way.

It means the Toolkit is alive enough to know what it does not yet know.

📑 Appendices & Templates

Purpose

This section collects reusable templates, field lists, relationship vocabularies, routing rules, and lightweight working structures used across the Toolkit.

The appendices should make the rest of the document easier to use.

They are not meant to be perfect or final. They are working materials that can be copied, adapted, simplified, or replaced as the Toolkit evolves.

1. Core Object Types

The Toolkit should preserve a small set of core object types.

Object type

Meaning

Primary home

Concept

An idea, term, or meaning structure.

Encyclopedia / Concept & Idea Ecology

Framework

A structured model for interpretation or action.

Encyclopedia / Concept & Idea Ecology

Resource

A link, article, book, paper, dataset, video, guide, or artifact.

Resource Graph

Source System

A living external knowledge environment, map, wiki, directory, forum, repo, or library.

Resource Graph

Organization

A formal or semi-formal group.

Resource Graph

Network

A distributed ecosystem or community field.

Resource Graph

Person

A thinker, builder, steward, researcher, organizer, maintainer, or contributor.

Resource Graph

Tool

A platform, app, protocol, library, or technical component.

Resource Graph / Infrastructure

Protocol

A formal system, standard, or technical coordination layer.

Resource Graph / Infrastructure

Standard

A shared specification, schema, or rule set.

Ontology / Interoperability

Option

A reusable design choice or pattern.

Option Library

Deployment

A context-specific assembly of options, tools, roles, boundaries, and operating structure.

Deployment & Structural Integrity

Implementation

What happens when a deployment is used in practice.

Implementation Memory

Signal

An observation from use, review, conflict, feedback, or implementation.

Implementation Memory / Evolution

Evidence

Material that supports, challenges, or contextualizes a claim.

Implementation Memory / Review

Pattern

A repeated or transferable lesson.

Option Library / Implementation Memory

Anti-pattern

A recurring design that tends to create harm, fragility, confusion, or hidden cost.

Implementation Memory / Evolution

Open Question

A question intentionally preserved for research or future sensemaking.

Open Questions & Research Backlog

Place

A city, region, watershed, bioregion, territory, site, or local context.

Resource Graph / Deployment

Claim

Something asserted about impact, function, evidence, status, or outcome.

Review / Implementation Memory

2. Routing Rules

Use these rules to decide where something belongs.

If it explains meaning

Route to:

  • Encyclopedia
  • Concept & Idea Ecology
  • Glossary

Examples:

  • What is source lineage?
  • What is regenerative web3?
  • What is a local node?
  • What is structural integrity?

If it is a real thing in the world

Route to:

  • Resource Graph & Ecosystem Atlas

Examples:

  • OpenCivics
  • Bloom Network
  • Karma GAP
  • Hypercerts
  • Decidim
  • farmOS
  • a paper
  • an event
  • a dataset
  • a person

If it is a reusable design choice

Route to:

  • Option Library

Examples:

  • review queue
  • source lineage field
  • milestone-based funding
  • adverse signal channel
  • local discovery map
  • claim-evidence record

If it is an operational use case

Route to:

  • Deployment & Structural Integrity

Examples:

  • a specific funding round design
  • a local node setup
  • a public resource map launch
  • an AI-assisted discovery workflow
  • a community directory

If it already happened

Route to:

  • Implementation Memory

Examples:

  • a completed event
  • a grant round
  • a workshop
  • a local campaign
  • a failed governance process
  • a tool experiment

If it is a lesson from practice

Route to:

  • Implementation Memory
  • Evolution
  • Option Library
  • Anti-patterns

Examples:

  • a repeated failure mode
  • a useful adaptation
  • a pattern from multiple cases
  • a deployment risk discovered through use

If it is unresolved

Route to:

  • Open Questions & Research Backlog
  • Unresolved Tension Log

Examples:

  • Should public maps include local communities?
  • What is enough evidence for an ecological claim?
  • How should AI-assisted recommendations be reviewed?

3. Resource Graph Entry Template

Full template

Field

Response

Name

URL / citation

Entry type

Organization, tool, person, project, event, framework, source system, etc.

Short description

Domain

Commons, ReFi, AI, localism, ecology, governance, funding, etc.

Primary function

Publish, map, coordinate, fund, verify, sensemake, discover, etc.

Related concepts

Related entities

Related Toolkit layers

Source lineage

Use type

Direct reference, summary, adaptation, synthesis, inspiration, implementation evidence.

Maturity status

Raw, candidate, reviewed, field-informed, deprecated, etc.

Review status

Needs review, source-reviewed, domain-reviewed, high-risk, etc.

Notes / risks

Open questions

Lightweight template

Field

Response

Name

Link

Type

Why it matters

Related concepts

Suggested placement

Source / contributor

4. Source System Template

Use this for living knowledge environments such as wikis, directories, maps, forums, repos, event archives, community libraries, and knowledge gardens.

Field

Response

Name

URL

Type

Wiki, map, directory, repository, forum, knowledge garden, archive, etc.

Steward / maintainer

What it curates

Primary domains

Relevant Toolkit layers

Useful entry points

How to credit

Reuse / adaptation notes

Correction path

Return path

Currentness / maintenance notes

Risks / caveats

Open questions

Source system questions

  • Is this a living knowledge system or a single resource?
  • Who maintains it?
  • What worldview, field, or community does it represent?
  • What should be credited if we use it?
  • Are we citing, summarizing, adapting, or synthesizing from it?
  • Does it contain sensitive or community-specific knowledge?
  • Should we ask for context before using it heavily?
  • How can value return to the source system?

5. Concept Entry Template

Field

Response

Concept

Short definition

Why it matters

Related lineages

Related resources

Related Toolkit layers

Common confusions

Tensions / critiques

Possible use

Encyclopedia page, Option Library entry, Deployment check, Resource Graph tag, etc.

Maturity status

Source lineage

Concept questions

  • What does this term mean in this Toolkit?
  • Who uses it differently?
  • Is it bridge language or structural language?
  • What lineages should be credited?
  • What risks arise if the term stays vague?
  • Should this become an Encyclopedia page?
  • Should it become a tag, type, or relationship?

6. Option Entry Template

Field

Response

Option name

Short description

Option family

Funding, governance / coordination, discovery, knowledge commons, etc.

Problem addressed

Useful when

Not useful when

Required roles

Required infrastructure

Required knowledge

Maintenance burden

Low, medium, high, context-dependent.

Related tools

Related concepts

Related cases

Compatibility notes

Incompatibility notes

Risks / failure modes

Consent / privacy notes

Power / control notes

Regenerative obligation notes

Evidence quality

Maturity status

Source lineage

Option questions

  • What design choice does this represent?
  • Is this actually an option, or is it a tool?
  • What conditions make it useful?
  • What conditions make it risky?
  • What roles must exist?
  • What hidden maintenance burden does it create?
  • Who could be harmed or excluded?
  • What tools can support it?
  • What cases show it working or failing?

7. Deployment Template

Field

Response

Deployment name

Purpose

Context

Scope

Participants

Cost-bearing parties

Selected options

Tools / components

Roles

Decision paths

Source of truth

Information requirements

Information asymmetries

Consent requirements

Power / control points

Adverse signal channels

Named response

Review cadence

Maintenance responsibilities

Evidence / reporting

Maturity status

Open questions

Lightweight deployment questions

  • What are we trying to do?
  • Who is involved?
  • Who could be affected?
  • What options or tools are we using?
  • Who decides what?
  • Where is the source of truth?
  • What consent is needed?
  • Who controls funds, data, infrastructure, or publication?
  • What can go wrong?
  • Where do concerns go?
  • Who responds?
  • What should we learn from this?

8. Structural Exposure Card

Use this when a deployment proceeds despite unresolved structural risk.

Field

Response

Deployment / object

Exposure identified

Affected parties

Severity

Low, medium, high, unknown.

Why proceeding may still make sense

Required disclosure

Named steward

Review date

Possible response

Revise, pause, narrow scope, add consent, add review, archive, continue with disclosure.

Example exposures

  • resource map includes communities without confirmed consent
  • AI-assisted recommendations are active before review
  • funding round has unclear appeal path
  • ecological claims lack sufficient evidence
  • public directory has no correction path
  • deployment depends on one overloaded maintainer
  • source system is used without clear attribution
  • local project is represented by outsiders
  • governance process names participation but decision power remains elsewhere

9. Place Profile Template

Use when a deployment, resource, case, or ecosystem map is local, regional, ecological, or bioregional.

Field

Response

Place name

Place type

City, region, watershed, bioregion, territory, site, neighborhood, etc.

Ecological context

Cultural / historical context

Current stewards

Existing projects

Local knowledge holders

Sensitive knowledge

Local assets

Local needs

Consent requirements

Visibility level

Public, community-visible, restricted, private, anonymized, etc.

Risks

Related resources

Review needs

Ecological, community, legal, cultural, structural, etc.

Place questions

  • Who knows this place from lived experience?
  • Who should be consulted?
  • What should not be mapped publicly?
  • What ecological context matters?
  • What social or historical context matters?
  • What local institutions or communities are active?
  • What risks come from visibility?
  • What would make this representation reciprocal?

10. Implementation Case Template

Field

Response

Name

Type

Local node, funding round, resource map, research sprint, workshop, etc.

Context

Participants

Purpose

Selected options

Tools / components

Source lineage

Timeline

Outputs

Outcomes

Signals

Failures / frictions

Adaptations

Evidence

Review status

Open questions

Toolkit updates

Lightweight implementation note

  • What happened?
  • Who was involved?
  • What was the purpose?
  • What tools or options were used?
  • What worked?
  • What did not work?
  • What surprised us?
  • What should be remembered?
  • What should update the Toolkit?

11. Retrospective Template

Question

Response

What did we try to do?

What actually happened?

What worked?

What did not work?

What created confusion?

What created energy?

What created friction?

What hidden labor appeared?

What assumptions changed?

What signals emerged?

What should be repeated?

What should not be repeated?

What should be updated in the Toolkit?

What remains unresolved?

Who should be credited?

Who should be consulted before sharing?

12. Signal Entry Template

Field

Response

Signal

Source

Implementation / context

Type

Learning, adverse, structural, capacity, ecological, funding, AI, etc.

Affected layer

Severity

Low, medium, high, unknown.

Who is affected

Interpretation

Response needed

None, review, update, escalation, pause, redesign, archive.

Status

Raw, under review, interpreted, acted on, unresolved.

Follow-up

13. Pattern Template

Field

Response

Pattern name

Problem context

Pattern summary

Conditions

Does not apply when

Examples

Evidence quality

Weak, medium, strong, mixed, anecdotal.

Related options

Related failure modes

Structural notes

Consent, power, review, roles, maintenance, etc.

Open questions

14. Anti-Pattern Template

Field

Response

Anti-pattern name

Short description

Symptoms

Why it appears attractive

Structural cause

Harms / risks

Examples / cases

Related options

Related concepts

Early warning signs

Possible mitigations

Review needs

Source lineage

Candidate anti-patterns

  • resource dump without stewardship
  • public map without consent
  • AI summary without source lineage
  • governance process without real decision power
  • token incentives without local trust
  • funding round without appeal path
  • impact claim without evidence
  • local node dependent on one organizer
  • public directory without correction path
  • review system without reviewer care
  • ecosystem map that credits aggregators but not sources
  • graph theater
  • platform lock-in
  • documentation without maintenance
  • regenerative branding without regenerative obligation

15. Open Question Template

Field

Response

Question

Why it matters

Related layer

Related concepts

Potential sources

Suggested method

Desk research, interview, ecosystem scan, case review, tool test, field experiment, etc.

Output

Priority

Low, medium, high, urgent.

Status

Open, in progress, partially answered, routed, archived.

Owner / steward

Notes

16. Review & Maturity States

Content maturity

State

Meaning

Raw

Added for visibility; not yet organized.

To-place

Useful but not yet placed in the right section.

Stub

Minimal entry; needs expansion.

Draft

Developed enough to discuss; not fully reviewed.

AI-assisted draft

Created or substantially shaped with AI; needs human review.

Source-linked

Has visible references or source lineage.

Candidate

Likely useful; needs validation or review.

Reviewed

Checked by relevant human reviewers.

Field-informed

Informed by real implementation or practice.

Pattern-generating

Has produced reusable learning across contexts.

Open question

Intentionally unresolved.

Deprecated

Retained for memory but no longer current or recommended.

Review types

Review type

Useful for

Source review

Citations, links, provenance.

Domain review

Accuracy within a field.

Structural review

Roles, obligations, risks, coordination assumptions.

Community review

Representation of a group or community.

Place-based review

Local, cultural, ecological, or bioregional context.

Legal / tax review

Jurisdiction-sensitive claims.

Ecological review

Environmental, MRV, biodiversity, or restoration claims.

AI review

AI-assisted synthesis, classification, or recommendations.

Implementation review

Practice-generated lessons.

17. Relationship Grammar

Basic relationships

  • created by
  • maintained by
  • stewarded by
  • funded by
  • hosted by
  • contributed to
  • cited by
  • adapted from
  • inspired by
  • used by
  • partnered with
  • active in
  • located in
  • part of
  • related to

Functional relationships

  • supports
  • enables
  • provides infrastructure for
  • helps map
  • helps publish
  • helps coordinate
  • helps fund
  • helps verify
  • helps sensemake
  • helps discover
  • helps review
  • helps implement
  • helps preserve memory

Knowledge relationships

  • defines
  • explains
  • compares
  • challenges
  • documents
  • synthesizes
  • preserves lineage of
  • provides evidence for
  • raises question about
  • contradicts
  • extends
  • translates
  • contextualizes

Design relationships

  • instantiates option
  • supports option
  • depends on
  • requires
  • constrains
  • complements
  • conflicts with
  • replaces
  • can be composed with
  • incompatible with
  • safer with
  • risky without

Implementation relationships

  • implemented by
  • tested in
  • generated signal for
  • produced evidence for
  • failed under
  • adapted through
  • created pattern for
  • revealed risk in
  • generated open question for

Stewardship relationships

  • obligated toward
  • accountable to
  • reviewed by
  • consented by
  • represented by
  • can be corrected by
  • should credit
  • should consult
  • should not expose
  • requires local review

18. Function Tags

Use function tags to describe what something helps people do.

Function

Meaning

Publish

Make knowledge or resources visible.

Store

Hold data, records, files, or knowledge.

Curate

Select, organize, classify, or maintain knowledge.

Search

Help people find information.

Map

Visualize relationships, places, systems, or ecosystems.

Explain

Clarify concepts or frameworks.

Convene

Bring people together.

Coordinate

Organize action, roles, or work.

Decide

Support decision-making or governance / coordination.

Fund

Allocate money or resources.

Verify

Support claims, evidence, attestations, or review.

Measure

Support metrics, MRV, monitoring, or evaluation.

Sensemake

Interpret complexity, disagreement, or feedback.

Discover

Find resources, communities, collaborators, or opportunities.

Match

Connect needs, offers, skills, projects, or people.

Protect

Support privacy, security, consent, or safety.

Learn

Support education, onboarding, or learning paths.

Remember

Preserve history, lessons, and implementation memory.

Evolve

Support updates, adaptation, and learning loops.

19. Bridge Language Table

Some terms are useful for communication but should be clarified when structure matters.

Bridge language

More precise structural language

Governance

Coordination architecture / coordination instrument

Accountability

Stewardship obligation / obligation direction

Stakeholders

Participants / contributors / affected parties / cost-bearing parties

Transparency

Multi-directional visibility

Enforcement

Detection with named response

Compliance

Conformance

Feedback

Signal / adverse signal / learning signal

Users

Participants / contributors / affected parties

Power structure

Power, control, and obligation structure

Rules

Structural conditions

Impact

Claim + evidence + context + uncertainty

Community

Specific group with boundaries, context, and representation questions

Ecosystem

Set of related actors, resources, concepts, tools, and environments

Decentralization

Specific distribution of control, authority, infrastructure, or risk

Regeneration

Contextual practice that should specify living system, place, relationship, and obligation

20. Claim-Evidence Table

Use this whenever the Toolkit makes or evaluates an important claim.

Claim

Evidence

Confidence

Source

Review status

Notes

Low / medium / high / unknown

Claim questions

  • What exactly is being claimed?
  • Is the claim conceptual, social, ecological, financial, operational, or technical?
  • What evidence supports it?
  • What evidence is missing?
  • Who collected the evidence?
  • Who reviewed it?
  • What uncertainty remains?
  • What should not be inferred?

21. Change Log Template

Date

Change

Reason

Source / signal

Contributor

Follow-up

Change log questions

  • What changed?
  • Why did it change?
  • What signal triggered the change?
  • Which sections were affected?
  • Was source lineage updated?
  • Was maturity status changed?
  • Is follow-up needed?

22. First-Pass Contributor Guide

Ways to contribute

A useful contribution can be small.

Examples:

  • add a resource
  • add a source system
  • add a concept
  • improve a definition
  • add a relationship
  • add source lineage
  • flag a weak claim
  • add an open question
  • add a failure mode
  • suggest a tool
  • add a case
  • improve a template
  • identify duplication
  • mark a risk
  • suggest a better category

Contribution questions

Before adding something, ask:

  • What is this?
  • Why does it matter?
  • Where does it belong?
  • What source should be credited?
  • What concepts does it connect to?
  • What function does it serve?
  • Does it involve people, communities, places, claims, or sensitive information?
  • Does it require review?
  • Is it a resource, concept, option, deployment, implementation, or open question?

Minimum contribution format

Field

Response

What I am adding

Link / source

Why it matters

Suggested section

Related concepts

Any risks or review needs

23. One-Page Summary Template

Use this when a section, source system, option, or implementation needs a concise overview.

Field

Response

Name

What it is

Why it matters

Key concepts

Relevant resources

Toolkit relevance

Risks / tensions

Open questions

Next steps

24. Minimum Viable Commons Checklist

A lightweight knowledge commons should have:

  • clear purpose
  • clear audience
  • source of truth
  • contribution path
  • source lineage field
  • resource entry template
  • concept entry template
  • review / maturity labels
  • open-question area
  • maintenance rhythm
  • change log
  • export or backup path
  • named stewards
  • unresolved tension log
  • pathway for implementation learning

Optional but useful:

  • Resource Graph table
  • glossary
  • relationship grammar
  • Option Library
  • deployment checklist
  • implementation case template
  • AI-use notes
  • privacy and consent guidance

25. Final Working Summary

These appendices are not separate from the Toolkit. They are reusable working instruments.

They help contributors:

  • classify entries
  • route content
  • preserve source lineage
  • compare options
  • prepare deployments
  • capture implementation memory
  • harvest patterns
  • document failure
  • update the commons
  • keep uncertainty visible

The templates should remain lightweight enough to use and structured enough to support future migration into tables, repositories, graphs, or other infrastructure.
